title.zh: 卷十一 劉玄劉盆子列傳
title.en: Volume 11: Biographies of Liu Xuan; Liu Penzi
title.raw: 卷十一 劉玄劉盆子列傳 Volume 11: Biographies of Liu Xuan; Liu Penzi

sentences.zh: 劉玄字聖公，光武族兄也。

translations.literal: Liu Xuan’s style was Shenggong; he was Guangwu’s male cousin on the clan line.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xuan, courtesy name Shenggong, was a kinsman of Liu Xiu, the future Emperor Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 弟為人所殺，聖公結客欲報之。

translations.literal: His younger brother was killed by someone; Shenggong gathered retainers wishing to avenge him.
translations.idiomatic: When his brother was murdered, he gathered swordsmen to take revenge.

sentences.zh: 客犯法，聖公避吏於平林。

translations.literal: The retainers broke the law; Shenggong fled the clerks at Pinglin.
translations.idiomatic: His men ran afoul of the law, and he fled official pursuit to Pinglin.

sentences.zh: 吏系聖公父子張。

translations.literal: The clerks bound Shenggong’s father and son Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: The authorities arrested his father Liu Zizhang and held him.

sentences.zh: 聖公詐死，使人持喪歸舂陵，吏乃出子張，聖公因自逃匿。

translations.literal: Shenggong feigned death, sent men bearing the bier to return to Chunling; the clerks then released Zizhang; Shenggong thereby himself fled and hid.
translations.idiomatic: He staged his own death and sent a mock funeral cortege to Chunling; when the jailers released his father, he slipped away into hiding.

sentences.zh: 王莽末，南方饑饉，人庶群入野澤，掘鳧茈而食之，更相侵奪。

translations.literal: At the end of Wang Mang, the south had famine; people in masses entered marshes and wilds, dug wild taro and ate them, mutually seizing from one another.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Wang Mang’s reign famine drove the southern populace into the wetlands to dig arrowhead tubers, and they fought one another for food.

sentences.zh: 新市人王匡、王鳳為平理諍訟，遂推為渠帥，眾數百人。

translations.literal: Xinshi men Wang Kuang and Wang Feng for them settled quarrels and lawsuits; then they were pushed as band chiefs, a crowd of several hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Kuang and Wang Feng of Xinshi settled disputes among the refugees and were chosen as leaders of several hundred men.

sentences.zh: 於是諸亡命馬武、王常、成丹等往從之；

translations.literal: Thereupon various fugitives Ma Wu, Wang Chang, Cheng Dan, and others went and followed them;
translations.idiomatic: Outlaws such as Ma Wu, Wang Chang, and Cheng Dan rallied to them;

sentences.zh: 共攻離鄉聚，臧於綠林中，數月間至七八千人。

translations.literal: together they attacked detached hamlets, stored themselves in the green forest; within several months they reached seven or eight thousand men.
translations.idiomatic: together they raided country settlements and took refuge in the Lulin marshes until their strength grew to seven or eight thousand within a few months.

sentences.zh: 地皇二年，荊州牧某發奔命二萬人攻之，匡等相率迎擊於雲杜，大破牧軍，殺數千人，盡獲輜重，遂攻拔竟陵。

translations.literal: In the second year of Dihuang, the Jingzhou shepherd so-and-so issued emergency call-up twenty thousand men to attack them; Kuang and others together led a welcoming strike at Yundu, greatly broke the shepherd’s army, killed several thousand, wholly captured baggage trains, then attacked and took Jingling.
translations.idiomatic: In 21 the Jingzhou governor mobilized twenty thousand emergency troops; Wang Kuang met them at Yundu, shattered the government force, killed thousands, seized the baggage train, and went on to capture Jingling.

sentences.zh: 轉擊雲杜、安陸，多略婦女，還入綠林中，至有五萬餘口，州郡不能制。

translations.literal: They turned to strike Yundu and Anlu, greatly abducted women, returned into the green forest, reaching more than fifty thousand persons; prefectures and commanderies could not control them.
translations.idiomatic: They struck Yundu and Anlu, carried off many women, and withdrew again to Lulin until their host exceeded fifty thousand—beyond the power of local officials to restrain.

sentences.zh: 三年，大疾疫，死者且半，乃各分散引去。

translations.literal: In the third year, great pestilence; the dead were nearly half; then each scattered and led away.
translations.idiomatic: The next year plague killed nearly half of them, and the bands broke up and dispersed.

sentences.zh: 王常、成丹西入南郡，號下江兵；

translations.literal: Wang Chang and Cheng Dan entered west into Nan commandery, called the Lower Yang army;
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang and Cheng Dan marched west into Nan commandery as the “Lower Yang” column;

sentences.zh: 王匡、王鳳、馬武及其支常朱鮪、張卬等北入南陽，號新市兵：皆自稱將軍。

translations.literal: Wang Kuang, Wang Feng, Ma Wu, and their branch Chang Zhu Wei, Zhang Ang, and others entered north into Nanyang, called the Xinshi army: all called themselves generals.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Kuang, Wang Feng, Ma Wu, Zhu Wei, Zhang Ang, and others went north into Nanyang as the “Xinshi” band, each styling himself general.

sentences.zh: 七月，匡等進攻隨，未能下。

translations.literal: In the seventh month, Kuang and others advanced to attack Sui, could not take it.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh month they besieged Sui but could not reduce the town.

sentences.zh: 平林人陳牧、廖湛復聚眾千餘人，號平林兵，以應之。

translations.literal: Pinglin men Chen Mu and Liao Zhan again gathered a crowd over a thousand, called the Pinglin army, to respond to them.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Mu and Liao Zhan of Pinglin raised another thousand men, the “Pinglin” force, to join the rebellion.

sentences.zh: 聖公因往從牧等，為其軍安集掾。

translations.literal: Shenggong thereupon went and followed Mu and others, becoming their army’s pacification and gathering clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xuan then joined Chen Mu’s band as clerk for pacification and muster.

sentences.zh: 更始忌伯升威名，遂誅之，以光祿勛劉賜為大司徒。

translations.literal: Gengshi envied Bosheng’s awesome name, then executed him, making Grand Counsellor of the Household Liu Ci Grand Minister over the Masses.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Gengshi, jealous of Liu Yan’s renown, had him killed and appointed Liu Ci grand minister over the masses.

sentences.zh: 前鐘武侯劉望起兵，略有汝南。

translations.literal: Former Marquis of Zhongwu Liu Wang raised troops, broadly took Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Wang, former marquis of Zhongwu, rebelled and overran Runan.

sentences.zh: 時王莽納言將軍嚴尤、秩宗將軍陳藏既敗於昆陽，往歸之。

translations.literal: At the time Wang Mang’s Receipt-of-Words General Yan You and Minister of the Masses General Chen Cang having been defeated at Kunyang, went to join him.
translations.idiomatic: Yan You and Chen Cang, Wang Mang’s generals who had lost at Kunyang, fled to Liu Wang’s camp.

sentences.zh: 八月，望遂自立為天子，以尤為大司馬、茂為丞相。

translations.literal: In the eighth month, Wang thereupon established himself as Son of Heaven, making You Grand Marshal and Mao Chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: In August Liu Wang declared himself emperor, named Yan You grand marshal and Chen Mao chancellor.

sentences.zh: 王莽使太師王匡、國將哀章守洛陽。

translations.literal: Wang Mang ordered Grand Tutor Wang Kuang and State General Ai Zhang to guard Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang sent Wang Kuang and Ai Zhang to hold Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 更始遣定國上公王匡攻洛陽，西屏大將軍申屠建、丞相司值李松攻武關，三輔震動。

translations.literal: Gengshi sent Stabilizing-the-State Supreme Duke Wang Kuang to attack Luoyang, Western Screen Grand General Shentu Jian and Chancellor’s Clerk of Duty Li Song to attack Wu Pass; the Three Adjuncts trembled.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi ordered Wang Kuang to take Luoyang and Shentu Jian and Li Song to storm Wu Pass; the capital region shook.

sentences.zh: 是時海內豪桀翕然響應，皆殺其牧守，自稱將軍，用漢年號，以待詔命，旬月之間，遍於天下。

translations.literal: At this time heroes within the seas together responded, all killed their shepherds and guards, called themselves generals, used Han reign titles, to await edict command; within ten days and a month, it spread over the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Bold men everywhere rose at once, slew their governors, styled themselves generals, and adopted Han reign years while awaiting Gengshi’s orders—within weeks the movement covered the realm.

sentences.zh: 長安中起兵攻未央官。

translations.literal: Within Chang’an troops rose to attack the Weiyang official.
translations.idiomatic: Rebels in Chang’an stormed the Weiyang Palace.

sentences.zh: 九月，東海人公賓就斬王莽於漸臺，收璽綬，傳首詣宛。

translations.literal: In the ninth month, Donghai man Gongbin Jiu beheaded Wang Mang on the Gradual Terrace, collected seals and ribbons, sent the head relayed to Wan.
translations.idiomatic: In September Gongbin Jiu cut down Wang Mang on the terrace by the imperial pool, seized the imperial seals, and sent his head to Wan.

sentences.zh: 更始時在便坐黃堂，取視之，喜曰：『莽不如是，當與霍光等。

translations.literal: Gengshi at the time was in the side hall Yellow Hall; he took and looked at it, joyfully saying, ‘If Mang had not been thus, he ought to have ranked with Huo Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi was in the side hall when the head arrived; he gloated that had Wang Mang not come to this, he might have rivaled Huo Guang.

sentences.zh: 』寵姬韓夫人笑曰：『若不如是，帝焉得之乎？

translations.literal: ’ His favored concubine Lady Han laughed, saying, ‘If it were not thus, how would Your Majesty have obtained it?
translations.idiomatic: His favorite, Lady Han, laughed and asked how else he could have gained the throne.

sentences.zh: 』更始悅，乃懸莽首於宛城市。

translations.literal: ’ Gengshi was pleased, then hung Mang’s head in Wan’s market.
translations.idiomatic: Pleased, he had Wang Mang’s head displayed in the marketplace at Wan.

sentences.zh: 是月，拔洛陽，生縛王匡、哀章，至，皆斬之。

translations.literal: That month they took Luoyang, bound alive Wang Kuang and Ai Zhang; when they arrived, all beheaded them.
translations.idiomatic: The same month Luoyang fell; Wang Kuang and Ai Zhang were taken alive and executed on arrival.

sentences.zh: 十月，使奮威大將軍劉信擊殺劉望於汝南，並誅嚴尤、陳茂。

translations.literal: In the tenth month, he sent Grand General Who Displays Might Liu Xin to strike and kill Liu Wang at Runan, and together executed Yan You and Chen Mao.
translations.idiomatic: In October he sent Liu Xin to destroy Liu Wang at Runan and put Yan You and Chen Mao to death as well.

sentences.zh: 更始遂北都洛陽，以劉賜為丞相。

translations.literal: Gengshi thereupon north made capital at Luoyang, making Liu Ci Chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi then moved his capital north to Luoyang and made Liu Ci chancellor.

sentences.zh: 申屠建、李松自長安傳送乘輿服御，又遣中黃門從官奉迎遷都。

translations.literal: Shentu Jian and Li Song from Chang’an relay-sent imperial equipage and carriages; again he sent Attendants at the Yellow Gates of the Center and follower officials to welcome the move of capital.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jian and Li Song sent the imperial carriages and regalia from Chang’an, and eunuch attendants were dispatched to escort the court to its new seat.

sentences.zh: 二年二月，更始自洛陽而西。

translations.literal: In the second year’s second month, Gengshi from Luoyang went west.
translations.idiomatic: In the second month of the second year of his reign he traveled west from Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 初發，李松奉引，馬驚奔，觸北宮鐵柱門，三馬皆死。

translations.literal: At first departure, Li Song led the traces; horses startled and bolted, struck the North Palace iron pillar gate; three horses all died.
translations.idiomatic: As the procession set out, Li Song led the team; the horses bolted into the iron gate of the North Palace and all three were killed.

sentences.zh: 初，王莽敗，唯未央宮被焚而已，其餘宮館一無所毀。

translations.literal: At first, when Wang Mang was defeated, only Weiyang Palace was burned; the rest of palaces and lodges nothing was destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang fell, only the Weiyang Palace had burned; every other hall stood intact.

sentences.zh: 宮女數千，備列後庭，自鐘鼓、帷帳、輿輦、器服、太倉、武庫、官府、市裏，不改於舊。

translations.literal: Palace women several thousand, fully arrayed in the rear court; from bells and drums, curtains and canopies, carriages and litters, vessels and dress, grand granary, armory, official bureaus, market wards—not changed from the old.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of palace ladies still waited in the harem; bells, hangings, chariots, wardrobes, the imperial granary and armory, offices, and market wards—all unchanged from Wang Mang’s day.

sentences.zh: 更始既至，居長樂宮，升前殿，郎吏以次列庭中。

translations.literal: When Gengshi arrived, he dwelt at Changle Palace, ascended the front hall, gentlemen clerks in order arrayed in the courtyard.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi took up residence in the Changle Palace, mounted the front hall, and court gentlemen lined the courtyard in order.

sentences.zh: 更始羞怍，俯首刮席不敢視。

translations.literal: Gengshi was ashamed and abashed, bowed head scraping the mat, did not dare look.
translations.idiomatic: Overcome with embarrassment, he kept his head down and traced the matting with his finger, unable to meet their eyes.

sentences.zh: 諸將後至者，更始問虜掠得幾何，左右侍官皆宮省久吏，各驚相視。

translations.literal: For the generals who arrived afterward, Gengshi asked how much plunder they had taken; left and right attendant officials were all long-time clerks of palace and ministry, each startled looked at one another.
translations.idiomatic: When latecomers among the generals arrived, he asked how much loot they had seized; the veteran chamberlains exchanged shocked glances.

sentences.zh: 李松與棘陽人趙萌說更始，宜悉王諸功臣。

translations.literal: Li Song together with Jiyang man Zhao Meng urged Gengshi, it was fitting wholly to enfeoff as kings the various merit ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Li Song and Zhao Meng of Jiyang urged Gengshi to enfeoff all his chief followers as kings.

sentences.zh: 朱鮪爭之，以為高祖約，非劉氏不王。

translations.literal: Zhu Wei disputed it, holding that by Gaozu’s covenant, none not of the Liu clan may be king.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Wei objected, citing Gaozu’s oath that only Liu clansmen might receive the title of king.

sentences.zh: 更始乃先封宗室太常將軍劉祉為定陶王、劉賜為宛王、劉慶為燕王、劉歙為元氏王、大將軍劉嘉為漢中王、劉信為汝陰王，後遂立王匡為比陽王、王鳳為宜城王、朱鮪為膠東王、衛尉大將軍張卬為淮陽王、廷尉大將軍王常為鄧王，執金吾大將軍廖湛為穰王、申屠建為平氏王、尚書胡殷為隨王、柱天大將軍李通為西平王、五威中郎將李軼為舞陰王、水衡大將軍成丹為襄邑王、大司空陳牧為陰平王、驃騎大將軍宋佻為潁陰王、尹尊為郾王。

translations.literal: Gengshi then first enfeoffed imperial clansman Grand Minister of Ceremonies General Liu Zhi as King of Dingtao, Liu Ci as King of Wan, Liu Qing as King of Yan, Liu Xi as King of Yuanshi, Grand General Liu Jia as King of Hanzhong, Liu Xin as King of Ruoyin; afterward then established Wang Kuang as King of Biyang, Wang Feng as King of Yicheng, Zhu Wei as King of Jiaodong, Commandant of the Guards Grand General Zhang Ang as King of Huaiyang, Commandant of Justice Grand General Wang Chang as King of Deng, Bearer of the Mace Grand General Liao Zhan as King of Rang, Shentu Jian as King of Pingshi, Minister Hu Yin as King of Sui, Pillar-of-Heaven Grand General Li Tong as King of Xiping, Five-Awesomeness Gentleman of the Household Li Yi as King of Wuyin, Superintendent of Waters Grand General Cheng Dan as King of Xiangyi, Grand Minister of Works Chen Mu as King of Yinping, General of Agile Cavalry Song Tiao as King of Yingyin, Yin Zun as King of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi first created kings among Liu kinsmen—Liu Zhi of Dingtao, Liu Ci of Wan, Liu Qing of Yan, Liu Xi of Yuanshi, Liu Jia of Hanzhong, and Liu Xin of Ruyin—then enfeoffed his generals: Wang Kuang at Biyang, Wang Feng at Yicheng, Zhu Wei in Jiaodong, Zhang Ang in Huaiyang, Wang Chang in Deng, Liao Zhan at Rang, Shentu Jian at Pingshi, Hu Yin at Sui, Li Tong at Xiping, Li Yi at Wuyin, Cheng Dan at Xiangyi, Chen Mu at Yinping, Song Tiao at Yingyin, and Yin Zun at Yan.

sentences.zh: 唯朱鮪辭曰：『臣非劉宗，不敢幹典。

translations.literal: Only Zhu Wei declined, saying, ‘Your servant is not of the Liu clan; I dare not infringe the canon.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Wei alone demurred that he was not of the Liu house and could not break the statute.

sentences.zh: 』遂讓不受。

translations.literal: ’ Then he yielded and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: He refused the title.

sentences.zh: 乃徙鮪為左大司馬，劉賜為前大司馬，使與李軼、李通、王常等鎮撫關東。

translations.literal: Then they moved Wei to Left Grand Marshal, Liu Ci to Front Grand Marshal, sent them together with Li Yi, Li Tong, Wang Chang, and others to pacify and soothe east of the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Wei was made left grand marshal instead, Liu Ci front grand marshal, and they were sent east of the passes with Li Yi, Li Tong, and Wang Chang to hold the region.

sentences.zh: 以李松為丞相，趙萌為右大司馬，共秉內任。

translations.literal: They made Li Song Chancellor, Zhao Meng Right Grand Marshal, together grasping inner responsibility.
translations.idiomatic: Li Song was named chancellor and Zhao Meng right grand marshal to manage affairs at the capital.

sentences.zh: 更始納趙萌女為夫人，有寵，遂委政於萌，日夜與婦人飲宴後庭。

translations.literal: Gengshi took in Zhao Meng’s daughter as lady, had favor, then entrusted government to Meng, day and night with women drinking feasts in the rear court.
translations.idiomatic: He took Zhao Meng’s daughter as consort, doted on her, and left government to her father while he feasted with women in the harem day and night.

sentences.zh: 群臣欲言事，輒醉不能見，時不得已，乃令侍中坐帷內與語。

translations.literal: When the host of ministers wished to speak on affairs, he was always drunk and could not see them; when at times there was no choice, then he ordered a Palace Attendant to sit inside the curtain and speak with them.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers who sought audience found him too drunk to receive them; when business could not wait, a palace attendant spoke from behind a curtain in his stead.

sentences.zh: 諸將識非更始聲，出皆怨曰：「成敗未可知，遽自縱放若此！

translations.literal: The generals recognized it was not Gengshi’s voice; leaving they all resentfully said, ‘Success or failure cannot yet be known—to so hastily indulge and release oneself thus!
translations.idiomatic: The generals knew the voice was not the emperor’s; leaving court they grumbled that the issue of victory was still open and he had no business debauching himself so soon.

sentences.zh: 」韓夫人尤嗜酒，每侍飲，見常侍奏事，輒怒曰：「帝方對我飲，正用此時持事來乎！

translations.literal: ’ Lady Han especially loved wine; whenever she attended drinking, seeing a Regular Attendant memorialize affairs, then angrily said, ‘The emperor is just facing me drinking—is this exactly the time to bring affairs?
translations.idiomatic: Lady Han was a drunkard: whenever a eunuch tried to present papers during their revels she would shout that the emperor was drinking with her and demand to know why they picked that moment for state business.

sentences.zh: 」起，抵破書案，趙萌專權，威福自己。

translations.literal: ’ She rose, smashed against and broke the writing desk; Zhao Meng monopolized power, awesomeness and blessing self-determined.
translations.idiomatic: She would smash the document stand; Zhao Meng ruled unchecked and wielded reward and punishment as he pleased.

sentences.zh: 郎吏有說萌放縱者，更始怒，拔劍擊之。

translations.literal: When a gentleman clerk spoke of Meng’s looseness, Gengshi was angry, drew sword and struck him.
translations.idiomatic: A court gentleman who criticized Zhao Meng’s excesses drew the emperor’s sword across his body in fury.

sentences.zh: 自是無復敢言。

translations.literal: From this none again dared speak.
translations.idiomatic: After that no one dared remonstrate.

sentences.zh: 萌私忿侍中，引下斬之，更始救請，不從。

translations.literal: Meng in private resentment toward a Palace Attendant, led him down and beheaded him; Gengshi pleaded rescue, did not comply.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Meng had a private grudge against a palace attendant, dragged him out and executed him, and ignored Gengshi’s pleas for mercy.

sentences.zh: 時李軼、朱鮪擅命山東，王匡、張卬橫暴三輔。

translations.literal: At the time Li Yi and Zhu Wei wielded orders east of the mountains; Wang Kuang and Zhang Ang were violent and oppressive in the Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yi and Zhu Wei did as they pleased east of the passes while Wang Kuang and Zhang Ang terrorized the capital region.

sentences.zh: 其所授官爵者，皆群小賈豎，或有膳夫庖人，多著繡面衣、錦褲、襜褕、諸於，罵詈道中。

translations.literal: Those to whom they granted office and rank were all petty peddlers, or some cooks and kitchen men; many wore embroidered face-cloths, brocade trousers, chan-yu, zhu-yu garments, and cursed on the roads.
translations.idiomatic: Their appointees were peddlers and cooks tricked out in embroidered jackets, brocade trousers, and long gowns who swore in the streets.

sentences.zh: 長安為之語曰：「竈下養，中郎將。

translations.literal: Chang’an made a saying on it, saying, ‘The kitchen-hand becomes a Gentlemen of the Household commander.
translations.idiomatic: Chang’an joked that promoting the stove-tender made a commander of the gentlemen of the household.

sentences.zh: 爛羊胃，騎都尉。

translations.literal: Rotten sheep stomach, Colonel of Attendant Cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Give him a rotten sheep’s stomach—he’s colonel of attendant cavalry.

sentences.zh: 爛羊頭，關內侯。

translations.literal: Rotten sheep head, Marquis within the Passes.
translations.idiomatic: Give him a rotten sheep’s head and he was marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 」軍帥將軍豫章李淑上書諫曰：「方今賊寇始誅，王化未行，百官有司宜慎其任。

translations.literal: ’ Army Marshal General of Yuzhang Li Shu submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying, ‘Now bandits and rebels have just been executed, kingly transformation has not yet been carried out; the hundred offices and responsible officials ought to be careful in their duties.
translations.idiomatic: Army marshal Li Shu of Yuzhang memorialized: the rebels were barely crushed, civil government not yet restored, and every office needed worthy appointees.

sentences.zh: 夫三公上應臺宿，九卿下括河海，故天工人其代之。

translations.literal: Now the Three Excellencies above correspond to terrace asterisms, the Nine Ministers below embrace rivers and seas; therefore Heaven’s work for man, others replace it for him.
translations.idiomatic: The three excellencies mirror the constellations of the Three Terraces; the nine ministers, like the nine provinces, carry Heaven’s mandate for the ruler.

sentences.zh: 陛下定業，雖因下江、平林之勢，斯蓋臨時濟用，不可施之既安。

translations.literal: Your Majesty’s settling the enterprise, although by the Lower Yang and Pinglin forces’ momentum—this is only temporary expedient use; it cannot be applied once already secure.
translations.idiomatic: Your majesty seized power with the Lower Yang and Pinglin bands—that was a wartime expedient, not a model for peacetime rule.

sentences.zh: 宜厘改制度，更延英俊，因才授爵，以匡王國。

translations.literal: It is fitting to rectify and change institutions, again summon outstanding talents, according to talent grant ranks, thereby to aid the royal state.
translations.idiomatic: Reform the institutions, summon able men, and match rank to talent so the dynasty may be governed.

sentences.zh: 今公卿大位莫非戎陳，尚書顯官皆出庸伍，資亭長、賊捕之用，而當輔佐綱維之任。

translations.literal: Now the great posts of dukes and ministers are none not from the martial array; eminent offices of ministers all come from vulgar ranks, relying on village head and thief-catcher’s use, yet bearing assistant-support and net-cord’s duties.
translations.idiomatic: Today’s ministers are camp followers, senior clerks are plucked from the ranks of petty officers fit only to be village headmen or constables, yet they are asked to uphold the state.

sentences.zh: 唯名與器，聖人所重。

translations.literal: Only name and regalia the sage esteems.
translations.idiomatic: Titles and insignia are what the sage treats as sacred.

sentences.zh: 今以所重加非其人，望其毗益萬分，興化致理，譬猶緣木求魚，升山采珠。

translations.literal: Now to take what is esteemed and add it to those not such persons, hoping they will assist and benefit in the least part, raise transformation and bring order—it is like climbing a tree to seek fish, ascending a mountain to gather pearls.
translations.idiomatic: To heap those honors on unworthy men and expect even a fraction of good government is like fishing in a tree or hunting pearls on a mountaintop.

sentences.zh: 海內望此，有以窺度漢祚。

translations.literal: Within the seas gazing on this, there is means to spy and measure Han’s mandate.
translations.idiomatic: The empire watches and takes the measure of Han’s fate from this.

sentences.zh: 臣非有憎疾以求進也，但為陛下惜此舉厝。

translations.literal: Your servant does not have hatred to seek advancement; only for Your Majesty I pity this course of action.
translations.idiomatic: I do not speak from spite or ambition, only grief for the course Your Majesty has chosen.

sentences.zh: 敗材傷錦，所宜至慮。

translations.literal: Rotten timber injures brocade—this is what ought to be utmost concern.
translations.idiomatic: Bad timber spoils fine brocade—this deserves your deepest thought.

sentences.zh: 惟割既往廖妄之失，思隆周文濟濟之美。

translations.literal: Only cut off past reckless errors, ponder raising King Wen of Zhou’s many-and-magnificent beauty.
translations.idiomatic: Renounce the reckless mistakes already made and aspire to the stately order of King Wen of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 」更始怒，系淑詔獄。

translations.literal: ’ Gengshi was angry, bound Shu in the edict prison.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi flew into a rage and threw Li Shu into the imperial prison.

sentences.zh: 自是，關中離心，四方怨叛。

translations.literal: From this, within the passes hearts turned away; in four directions resentment and rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: After that the capital region lost heart and the provinces turned mutinous.

sentences.zh: 諸將出征，各自專置牧守，州郡交錯，不知所從。

translations.literal: When the various generals went out on campaign, each on his own monopolized setting shepherds and guards; prefectures and commanderies crisscrossed; none knew whom to follow.
translations.idiomatic: Generals on campaign appointed their own governors until jurisdictions overlapped and no one knew whom to obey.

sentences.zh: 十二月，赤眉西入關。

translations.literal: In the twelfth month, the Red Eyebrows entered west into the passes.
translations.idiomatic: In the twelfth month the Red Eyebrows marched west into the passes.

sentences.zh: 三年正月，平陵人方望立前孺子劉嬰為天子。

translations.literal: In the third year’s first month, Pingling man Fang Wang established the former Infant Liu Ying as Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: In the first month of the third year of Gengshi’s reign, Fang Wang of Pingling set up Liu Ying, the former child emperor, as sovereign.

sentences.zh: 初，望見更始政亂，度其必敗，謂安陵人弓林等曰：『前定安公嬰，平帝之嗣，雖王莽篡奪，而嘗為漢主。

translations.literal: At first, Wang saw Gengshi’s government in chaos, measured that he must fail, and said to Anling man Gong Lin and others, ‘The former Duke of Ding’an Ying, Emperor Ping’s heir, although Wang Mang usurped and seized, yet once was Han ruler.
translations.idiomatic: Fang Wang saw Gengshi’s rule collapsing and told Gong Lin of Anling that Liu Ying, though set aside by Wang Mang, had once been Han sovereign.

sentences.zh: 今皆云劉氏真人，當更受命，欲共定大功，何如？

translations.literal: Now all say the Liu clan are true men, ought again to receive the mandate; I wish together to settle great merit—how about it?
translations.idiomatic: Men said the house of Liu still held Heaven’s mandate—why not join in a great enterprise?

sentences.zh: 』林等然之，乃於長安求得嬰，將至臨涇立之。

translations.literal: ’ Lin and others deemed it right; then in Chang’an they sought and obtained Ying, were about to bring him to Linjing and establish him.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Lin agreed; they found Liu Ying in Chang’an and escorted him toward Linjing to enthrone him.

sentences.zh: 聚黨數千人，望為丞相，林為大司馬。

translations.literal: They gathered a faction of several thousand; Wang became Chancellor, Lin Grand Marshal.
translations.idiomatic: Several thousand rallied to them; Fang Wang took the title chancellor and Gong Lin grand marshal.

sentences.zh: 更始遣李松與討難將軍蘇茂等擊破，皆斬之。

translations.literal: Gengshi sent Li Song together with General Who Punishes Rebellion Su Mao and others to strike and break them; all beheaded them.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi sent Li Song and Su Mao to crush the rising and executed the leaders.

sentences.zh: 又使蘇茂拒赤眉於弘農，茂軍敗，死者千餘人。

translations.literal: Again he sent Su Mao to resist the Red Eyebrows at Hongnong; Mao’s army was defeated, more than a thousand dead.
translations.idiomatic: Su Mao was then sent to block the Red Eyebrows at Hongnong but lost a thousand men in defeat.

sentences.zh: 三月，遣李松會朱鮪與赤眉戰於蓩鄉，松等大敗，棄軍走，死者三萬餘人。

translations.literal: In the third month, he sent Li Song to join Zhu Wei in battle with the Red Eyebrows at Maoxiang; Song and others were greatly defeated, abandoned the army and fled; more than thirty thousand dead.
translations.idiomatic: In March Li Song joined Zhu Wei against the Red Eyebrows at Maoxiang, suffered a crushing defeat, fled the field, and lost more than thirty thousand men.

sentences.zh: 時王匡、張卬守河東，為鄧禹所破，還奔長安。

translations.literal: At the time Wang Kuang and Zhang Ang guarded Hedong, were broken by Deng Yu, returned fleeing to Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Kuang and Zhang Ang, holding Hedong, were routed by Deng Yu and fled back to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 卬與諸將議曰：『赤眉近在鄭、華陰間，旦暮且至。

translations.literal: Ang with the various generals deliberated, saying, ‘The Red Eyebrows are near between Zheng and Huayin; morning and evening they will arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ang urged his fellow generals: the Red Eyebrows were between Zheng and Huayin and would reach Chang’an at any hour.

sentences.zh: 今獨有長安，見滅不久，不如勒兵掠城中以自富，轉攻所在，東歸南陽，收宛王等兵。

translations.literal: Now we alone have Chang’an; seeing destruction not long—better to press troops and plunder the city to enrich ourselves, turn to attack wherever we are, east return to Nanyang, gather the Wan king and others’ troops.
translations.idiomatic: Chang’an alone could not long survive; better to loot the capital, strike out where they could, fall back east to Nanyang, and rally Liu Ci of Wan and other forces.

sentences.zh: 事若不集，復入湖池中為盜耳。

translations.literal: If the affair does not succeed, again enter lakes and marshes and be bandits, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: If the plan failed they could always return to the marshes as outlaws.

sentences.zh: 』申屠建、廖湛等皆以為然，共人說更始。

translations.literal: ’ Shentu Jian, Liao Zhan, and others all deemed it right, together persons persuaded Gengshi.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Jian and Liao Zhan agreed and went in a body to urge Gengshi.

sentences.zh: 更始怒不應，莫敢復言。

translations.literal: Gengshi was angry and did not answer; none dared again speak.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi refused angrily and no one dared press the point.

sentences.zh: 及赤眉立劉盆子，更始使王匡、陳牧、成丹、趙萌屯新豐，李松軍掫，以拒之。

translations.literal: When the Red Eyebrows established Liu Penzi, Gengshi ordered Wang Kuang, Chen Mu, Cheng Dan, and Zhao Meng to garrison Xinfeng, Li Song’s army at Zou, to resist them.
translations.idiomatic: When the Red Eyebrows enthroned Liu Penzi, Gengshi posted Wang Kuang, Chen Mu, Cheng Dan, and Zhao Meng at Xinfeng and Li Song at Zou to hold them off.

sentences.zh: 張卬、廖湛、胡殷、申屠建等與御史大夫隗囂合謀，欲以立秋日貙膢時共劫更始，俱成前計。

translations.literal: Zhang Ang, Liao Zhan, Hu Yin, Shentu Jian, and others together with Imperial Counsellor Wei Xiao plotted, wishing on the Beginning of Autumn day at the chu-lü sacrifice time jointly to seize Gengshi, together completing the former plan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ang, Liao Zhan, Hu Yin, and Shentu Jian conspired with Imperial Counsellor Wei Xiao to kidnap Gengshi at the autumn chu-lü rite and carry out their scheme to abandon Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 侍中劉能卿知其謀，以告之。

translations.literal: Palace Attendant Liu Nengqing knew their plot and reported it to him.
translations.idiomatic: Palace attendant Liu Nengqing learned of the plot and warned the emperor.

sentences.zh: 更始托病不出，召張卬等。

translations.literal: Gengshi feigned illness and did not go out, summoned Zhang Ang and others.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi pleaded illness, stayed in the palace, and summoned Zhang Ang and his confederates.

sentences.zh: 卬等皆入，將悉誅之，唯隗囂不至。

translations.literal: Ang and others all entered; he was about to execute them all; only Wei Xiao did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: They came as ordered, and he meant to kill them all—but Wei Xiao failed to appear.

sentences.zh: 更始狐疑，使卬等四人且待於外廬。

translations.literal: Gengshi hesitated, ordered Ang and the four others for the moment to wait in the outer lodge.
translations.idiomatic: Uncertain, he told the four to wait in an outer chamber.

sentences.zh: 卬與湛、殷疑有變，遂突出，獨申屠建在，更始斬之。

translations.literal: Ang together with Zhan and Yin suspected there was a change, then burst out; only Shentu Jian was present; Gengshi beheaded him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ang, Liao Zhan, and Hu Yin sensed a trap and bolted; Shentu Jian alone remained and was cut down.

sentences.zh: 卬與湛、殷遂勒兵掠東西市。

translations.literal: Ang together with Zhan and Yin thereupon pressed troops to plunder the eastern and western markets.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ang, Liao Zhan, and Hu Yin then marched on the eastern and western markets and looted them.

sentences.zh: 昏時，燒門入，戰於宮中，更始大敗。

translations.literal: At dusk they burned the gate and entered, battled in the palace; Gengshi was greatly defeated.
translations.idiomatic: At dusk they burned the gate, fought into the palace, and routed Gengshi’s guard.

sentences.zh: 明旦，將妻子車騎百餘，東奔越萌於新豐。

translations.literal: Next dawn, leading wife and children, chariots and riders over a hundred, he fled east to join Meng at Xinfeng.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn he fled east to Xinfeng with his family and a hundred horsemen to join Zhao Meng’s camp.

sentences.zh: 更始復疑王匡、陳牧、成丹與張卬等同謀，乃並召入。

translations.literal: Gengshi again suspected Wang Kuang, Chen Mu, and Cheng Dan were together plotting with Zhang Ang and others, then together summoned them in.
translations.idiomatic: Suspecting Wang Kuang, Chen Mu, and Cheng Dan of collusion with Zhang Ang, he summoned them as well.

sentences.zh: 牧、丹先至，即斬之。

translations.literal: Mu and Dan arrived first; at once they beheaded them.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Mu and Cheng Dan arrived first and were executed on the spot.

sentences.zh: 王匡懼，將兵入長安，與張卬等合。

translations.literal: Wang Kuang was afraid; he led troops into Chang’an and joined with Zhang Ang and others.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Kuang, in fear, marched into Chang’an and united with Zhang Ang.

sentences.zh: 李松還從更始，與趙萌共攻匡、卬於城內。

translations.literal: Li Song returned and followed Gengshi, together with Zhao Meng jointly attacked Kuang and Ang within the city.
translations.idiomatic: Li Song sided again with Gengshi and, with Zhao Meng, fought Wang Kuang and Zhang Ang inside the walls.

sentences.zh: 連戰月餘，匡等敗走，更始徙居長信宮。

translations.literal: They battled connectedly more than a month; Kuang and others were defeated and fled; Gengshi moved to dwell at Changxin Palace.
translations.idiomatic: After more than a month of fighting Wang Kuang fled, and Gengshi withdrew to the Changxin Palace.

sentences.zh: 赤眉至高陵，匡等迎降之，遂共連兵而進。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows reached Gaoling; Kuang and others went to meet and surrender to them; then together they joined troops and advanced.
translations.idiomatic: When the Red Eyebrows reached Gaoling, Wang Kuang surrendered and marched with them against Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 更始守城，使李松出戰，敗，死者二千餘人，赤眉生得松。

translations.literal: Gengshi defended the city, ordered Li Song to go out and battle; he was defeated, more than two thousand dead; the Red Eyebrows captured Song alive.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi sent Li Song to give battle; he lost two thousand men and was taken alive.

sentences.zh: 時松弟汎為城門校尉，赤眉使使謂之曰：『開城門，活汝兄。

translations.literal: At the time Song’s younger brother Fan was Colonel of the City Gates; the Red Eyebrows sent an envoy saying to him, ‘Open the city gates, spare your elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: Li Song’s brother Li Fan commanded the gates; the Red Eyebrows promised to spare Li Song if he opened them.

sentences.zh: 』汎即開門。

translations.literal: ’ Fan at once opened the gates.
translations.idiomatic: Li Fan opened the gates.

sentences.zh: 九月，赤眉入城。

translations.literal: In the ninth month, the Red Eyebrows entered the city.
translations.idiomatic: In September the Red Eyebrows entered Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 更始單騎走，從廚城門出，諸婦女從後連呼曰：『陛下，當下謝城！

translations.literal: Gengshi on a single horse fled, went out from the Kitchen City Gate; various women from behind continuously called, saying, ‘Your Majesty, ought to descend and thank the city!
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi fled alone through the Kitchen Gate while women behind him cried that he must dismount and bow to the city wall.

sentences.zh: 』更始即下拜，復上馬去。

translations.literal: ’ Gengshi at once descended and bowed, again mounted horse and left.
translations.idiomatic: He dismounted, made obeisance toward the walls, remounted, and rode on.

sentences.zh: 初，侍中劉恭以赤眉立其弟盆子，自系詔獄：聞更始敗，乃出，步從至高陵，止傳舍。

translations.literal: At first, Palace Attendant Liu Gong because the Red Eyebrows established his younger brother Penzi, himself bound in the edict prison: hearing Gengshi’s defeat, then came out, walked following to Gaoling, stopped at a relay inn.
translations.idiomatic: Palace attendant Liu Gong had imprisoned himself when the Red Eyebrows made his brother Penzi emperor; on news of Gengshi’s fall he walked to Gaoling and lodged at a post station.

sentences.zh: 右輔都尉嚴本恐失更始為赤眉所誅，將兵在外，號為屯衛而實囚之。

translations.literal: Right Adjunct Commandant Yan Ben feared losing Gengshi and being executed by the Red Eyebrows, led troops outside, called it garrison guard but in truth imprisoned him.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Ben, right-adjunct commandant, kept troops around Gengshi under pretense of guarding him but actually held him prisoner, fearing the rebels would kill him if he escaped.

sentences.zh: 赤眉下書曰：『聖公降者，封長沙王。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows issued a document saying, ‘Whoever Shenggong surrenders will be enfeoffed King of Changsha.
translations.idiomatic: The Red Eyebrows offered to enfeoff Liu Xuan as king of Changsha if he surrendered.

sentences.zh: 過二十日，勿受。

translations.literal: Passing twenty days, will not accept.
translations.idiomatic: The offer would lapse after twenty days.

sentences.zh: 』更始遣劉恭請降，赤眉使其將謝祿往受之。

translations.literal: ’ Gengshi sent Liu Gong to request surrender; the Red Eyebrows ordered their general Xie Lu to go receive it.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong sued for surrender on his behalf, and Xie Lu was sent to accept.

sentences.zh: 十月，更始遂隨祿肉袒詣長樂宮，上璽綬於盆子。

translations.literal: In the tenth month, Gengshi then followed Lu, bared upper body, went to Changle Palace, handed up seals and ribbons to Penzi.
translations.idiomatic: In October Liu Xuan went bare-chested to the Changle Palace with Xie Lu and surrendered the imperial seals to Liu Penzi.

sentences.zh: 赤眉坐更始，置庭中，將殺之。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows seated Gengshi, placed him in the courtyard, were about to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: They seated him in the courtyard and prepared to execute him.

sentences.zh: 劉恭、謝祿為請，不能得，遂引更始出。

translations.literal: Liu Gong and Xie Lu pleaded for him, could not obtain, then led Gengshi out.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong and Xie Lu begged for his life without success and led him away.

sentences.zh: 劉恭追呼曰：『臣誠力極，請得先死。

translations.literal: Liu Gong chased calling, saying, ‘Your servant’s strength is truly exhausted; I beg to obtain dying first.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong cried out that his strength was spent and begged to die first.

sentences.zh: 』拔劍欲自刎，赤眉帥樊崇等遽共救止之，乃赦更始，封為畏威侯。

translations.literal: ’ He drew sword wishing to cut his own throat; Red Eyebrows commander Fan Chong and others hastened together to rescue and stop him; then pardoned Gengshi, enfeoffed him as Marquis Who Fears Might.
translations.idiomatic: He drew his sword to kill himself; Fan Chong and the chiefs restrained him; Gengshi was spared and created marquis who fears might.

sentences.zh: 劉恭復為固請，竟得封長沙王。

translations.literal: Liu Gong again firmly pleaded, ultimately obtained enfeoffment as King of Changsha.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong pressed again until his brother was given the title king of Changsha.

sentences.zh: 更始常依謝祿居，劉恭亦擁護之。

translations.literal: Gengshi constantly relied on dwelling with Xie Lu; Liu Gong also shielded and protected him.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xuan lived under Xie Lu’s protection while Liu Gong watched over him.

sentences.zh: 三輔苦赤眉暴虐，皆憐更始，而張卬等以為慮，謂祿曰：『今諸營長多欲篡聖公者。

translations.literal: The Three Adjuncts suffered the Red Eyebrows’ violence and cruelty, all pitied Gengshi, but Zhang Ang and others took it as worry, saying to Lu, ‘Now the various camp chiefs mostly wish to usurp Shenggong.
translations.idiomatic: The capital people pitied Liu Xuan, but Zhang Ang and his faction warned Xie Lu that many camp chiefs meant to kill him and seize power.

sentences.zh: 一旦失之，合兵攻公，自滅之道也。

translations.literal: If once he is lost, they will join troops to attack you—the way of self-destruction.
translations.idiomatic: If Liu Xuan escaped their hands, they would turn their combined strength on Xie Lu—a path to ruin.

sentences.zh: 』於是祿使從兵與更始共牧馬於郊下，因令縊殺之。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon Lu ordered follower troops together with Gengshi jointly to herd horses below the suburbs, then ordered strangling to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Lu took Liu Xuan outside the city under pretense of grazing horses and had him strangled.

sentences.zh: 劉恭夜往收臧其屍。

translations.literal: Liu Gong at night went to gather and store his corpse.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong went by night to recover the body.

sentences.zh: 光武聞而傷焉。

translations.literal: Guangwu heard and grieved for it.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu grieved when he heard the news.

sentences.zh: 詔大司徒鄧禹葬之於霸陵。

translations.literal: He edicted Grand Minister over the Masses Deng Yu to bury him at Baling.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered Deng Yu to bury Liu Xuan at Baling.

sentences.zh: 有三子；

translations.literal: He had three sons;
translations.idiomatic: He had three sons:

sentences.zh: 求，歆，鯉。

translations.literal: Qiu, Xin, Li.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qiu, Liu Xin, and Liu Li.

sentences.zh: 明年夏，求兄弟與母東詣洛陽，帝封求為襄邑侯，奉更始祀；

translations.literal: The next summer, Qiu’s brothers together with their mother east went to Luoyang; the emperor enfeoffed Qiu as Marquis of Xiangyi, to serve Gengshi’s sacrifices;
translations.idiomatic: The next summer the brothers and their mother came east to Luoyang; the emperor made Liu Qiu marquis of Xiangyi to maintain Liu Xuan’s ancestral rites;

sentences.zh: 歆為穀孰侯，鯉為壽光侯。

translations.literal: Xin was Marquis of Gushu, Li was Marquis of Shouguang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin was made marquis of Gushu and Liu Li marquis of Shouguang.

sentences.zh: 求後徙封成陽侯。

translations.literal: Qiu afterward was moved enfeoffment to Marquis of Chengyang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Qiu was later transferred to the Chengyang marquisate.

sentences.zh: 求卒，子巡嗣，復徙封澤侯。

translations.literal: Qiu died; his son Xun succeeded; again was moved enfeoffment to Marquis of Ze.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Qiu died, his son Liu Xun inherited and was later moved to the Ze marquisate.

sentences.zh: 巡卒，子姚嗣。

translations.literal: Xun died; his son Yao succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xun was succeeded by his son Liu Yao.

sentences.zh: 論曰：周武王觀兵孟津，退而還師，以為紂未可伐，斯時有未至者也。

translations.literal: The treatise says: King Wu of Zhou reviewed troops at Meng Ford, withdrew and returned the army, holding that Zhou was not yet fit to attack—this was a time not yet arrived.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: King Wu of Zhou paraded at Meng Ford but turned back because the moment to strike Shang had not yet come.

sentences.zh: 漢起，驅輕黠烏合之眾，不當天下萬分之一，而旌旃之所撝及，書文之所通被，莫不折戈頓顙，爭受職命。

translations.literal: Han rose, driving light and cunning crow-flock masses, not matching one ten-thousandth of the empire, yet where banners and flags waved to, where documents and writing reached, none did not break spears and knock foreheads, striving to receive office and command.
translations.idiomatic: Han began with a rabble that was not one part in ten thousand of the realm, yet wherever its standards passed and its writ ran, enemies cast down arms and begged for posts.

sentences.zh: 非唯漢人餘思，固亦幾運之會也。

translations.literal: Not only Han people’s lingering thoughts—it was also the convergence of fortune’s timing.
translations.idiomatic: This owed not only to lingering loyalty to Han but to the turning of Heaven’s wheel.

sentences.zh: 夫為權首，鮮或不及。

translations.literal: Now to be head of power, few or none escape harm.
translations.idiomatic: Those who seize the lead in a crisis seldom escape the reckoning.

sentences.zh: 陳、項且猶未興，況庸庸者乎！

translations.literal: Chen and Xiang moreover had not yet risen—how much more ordinary mediocrities!
translations.idiomatic: Chen Sheng and Xiang Yu had not yet appeared—what hope for a mediocrity like Liu Xuan?

sentences.zh: 劉盆子

translations.literal: Liu Penzi.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Penzi

sentences.zh: 劉盆子者，太山式人，城陽景王章之後也。

translations.literal: As for Liu Penzi, he was a man of Shi in Taishan, a descendant of Prince Jing of Chengyang, Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Penzi came from Shi in Taishan and was a descendant of Prince Zhang of Chengyang, son of Emperor Jing of Han.

sentences.zh: 祖父憲，元帝時封為式侯，父萌嗣。

translations.literal: His grandfather Xian, in Emperor Yuan’s time was enfeoffed as Marquis of Shi; his father Meng succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Liu Xian had been marquis of Shi under Emperor Yuan; his father Liu Meng inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 王莽篡位，國除，因為式人焉。

translations.literal: Wang Mang usurped the throne; the state was abolished; thereby he became a man of Shi.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang abolished the fief, and the family became commoners of Shi.

sentences.zh: 天鳳元年，瑯邪海曲有呂母者，子為縣吏，犯小罪，宰論殺之。

translations.literal: In the first year of Tianfeng, in Langye’s Haiqu there was a woman Mother Lü; her son was a county clerk, committed a small crime, the magistrate judged and killed him.
translations.idiomatic: In 14, in Haiqu in Langye, a woman surnamed Lü had a son who was a county clerk; for a petty offense the magistrate had him executed.

sentences.zh: 呂母怨宰，密聚客，規以報仇。

translations.literal: Mother Lü resented the magistrate, secretly gathered guests, planned thereby to avenge.
translations.idiomatic: Mother Lü nursed a grudge and secretly gathered followers to plot revenge.

sentences.zh: 母家素豐，資產數百萬，乃益釀醇酒，買刀劍衣服。

translations.literal: The mother’s family was by nature rich, assets several million; then she increased brewing pure wine, bought swords and clothes.
translations.idiomatic: She was wealthy; she brewed fine wine and bought swords and clothes.

sentences.zh: 少年來酤者，皆賒與之，視其乏者，輒假衣裳，不問多少。

translations.literal: Youths who came to buy wine, all were given on credit; seeing those who lacked, then lent clothes, not asking how much.
translations.idiomatic: Young men who came for wine were never charged; she lent clothes to any in need without stint.

sentences.zh: 數年，財用稍盡，少年欲相與償之。

translations.literal: Several years, wealth and use gradually exhausted; the youths wished together to repay her.
translations.idiomatic: After some years her funds ran low and the young men offered to repay her.

sentences.zh: 呂母垂泣曰：『所以厚諸君者，非欲求利，徒以縣宰不道，枉殺吾子，欲為報怨耳。

translations.literal: Mother Lü weeping said, ‘The reason I treated you generously was not to seek profit, only because the county magistrate lacked the Way, wrongfully killed my son—I wished to avenge the grudge.
translations.idiomatic: Weeping, she told them she had not sought profit but only redress: the magistrate had murdered her son without cause.

sentences.zh: 諸君寧肯哀之乎！

translations.literal: Will you gentlemen pity me!
translations.idiomatic: Would they not pity her and help?

sentences.zh: 』少年壯其意，又素受恩，皆許諾。

translations.literal: ’ The youths deemed her purpose heroic, and moreover had long received kindness; all promised.
translations.idiomatic: Moved by her cause and her past kindness, they swore to help.

sentences.zh: 其中勇士自號猛虎，遂相聚得數十百人，因與呂母入海中，招合亡命，眾至數千。

translations.literal: Among them brave men called themselves Fierce Tigers; then gathering obtained several tens and hundreds of men; thereby with Mother Lü entered the sea, summoned and joined fugitives, the crowd reaching several thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Bold spirits styled themselves the Fierce Tigers; hundreds gathered and followed her to the coast to recruit outlaws until she commanded thousands.

sentences.zh: 呂母自稱將軍，引兵還攻破海曲，執縣宰。

translations.literal: Mother Lü called herself General, led troops back, broke and took Haiqu, seized the county magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: She styled herself general, stormed Haiqu, and seized the magistrate.

sentences.zh: 諸吏叩頭為宰請。

translations.literal: The various clerks knocked heads for the magistrate’s plea.
translations.idiomatic: His subordinates kowtowed and begged for his life.

sentences.zh: 母曰：『吾子犯小罪，不當死，而為宰所殺。

translations.literal: The mother said, ‘My son committed a small crime, ought not to have died, yet was killed by the magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: She said her son’s fault did not deserve death yet the magistrate had killed him.

sentences.zh: 殺人當死，又何請乎？

translations.literal: To kill a man ought to die—what plea is there?
translations.idiomatic: A murderer must die—why should she spare him?

sentences.zh: 』遂斬之，以其首祭子冢，復還海中。

translations.literal: ’ Then she beheaded him, with his head sacrificed at her son’s tomb, again returned to the sea.
translations.idiomatic: She beheaded him, offered his head at her son’s grave, and returned to her base on the coast.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，瑯邪人樊崇起兵於莒，眾百餘人，轉入太山，自號三老。

translations.literal: Several years later, Langye man Fan Chong raised troops at Ju, a crowd over a hundred, turned and entered Taishan, called himself Elder.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later Fan Chong of Langye raised a band of a hundred men at Ju, moved into Taishan, and styled himself elder.

sentences.zh: 時青、徐大饑，寇賊蜂起，眾盜以崇勇猛，皆附之，一歲間至萬餘人。

translations.literal: At the time Qing and Xu had great famine; bandits and robbers swarmed up; the mass of bandits because Chong was brave and fierce, all attached to him; within one year reached over ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Famine in Qing and Xu brought a swarm of bandits who rallied to Fan Chong’s courage until he had ten thousand men within a year.

sentences.zh: 崇同郡人逄安，東海人徐宣、謝祿、楊音，各起兵，合數萬人，復引從崇。

translations.literal: Chong’s same-commandery man Pang An, Donghai men Xu Xuan, Xie Lu, Yang Yin, each raised troops, together several tens of thousands, again led to follow Chong.
translations.idiomatic: Pang An of his commandery and Xu Xuan, Xie Lu, and Yang Yin of Donghai each raised bands that merged into tens of thousands under Fan Chong.

sentences.zh: 共還攻莒，不能下，轉掠至姑幕，因擊王莽探湯侯田況，大破之，殺萬餘人，遂北入青州，所過虜掠。

translations.literal: Together they returned to attack Ju, could not take it, turned to plunder reaching Gumu, then struck Wang Mang’s Scald-the-Broth Marquis Tian Kuang, greatly broke him, killed over ten thousand, then entered north into Qingzhou, wherever they passed plundering.
translations.idiomatic: They failed to take Ju, raided as far as Gumu, shattered Tian Kuang’s army, killed ten thousand, and swept north through Qingzhou, looting as they went.

sentences.zh: 還至太山，留屯南城。

translations.literal: Returning to Taishan, they stayed and garrisoned South City.
translations.idiomatic: They withdrew to Taishan and camped at the southern city.

sentences.zh: 初，崇等以困窮為冠，無攻城徇地之計。

translations.literal: At first, Chong and others because of hardship and poverty became bandits, had no plan to attack cities or seize land.
translations.idiomatic: At first they were starving outlaws with no plan to conquer cities or territory.

sentences.zh: 眾既浸盛，乃相與為約：殺人者死，傷人者償創。

translations.literal: When the crowd had gradually grown great, then together they made a covenant: killers die, wounders compensate wounds.
translations.idiomatic: As their numbers grew they swore a code: death for murder, restitution for wounding.

sentences.zh: 以言辭為約束，無文書、旌旗、部曲、號令。

translations.literal: They took words as binding; there were no documents, banners, units, or commands.
translations.idiomatic: They relied on word of mouth—no written orders, standards, formal units, or bugle calls.

sentences.zh: 其中最尊者號三老，次從事，次卒史，泛相稱曰巨人。

translations.literal: Among them the most honored were titled Elder; next Clerk; next Sergeant; loosely they called one another Giant.
translations.idiomatic: Their highest rank was elder, then clerk, then sergeant; they loosely called one another “giant.”

sentences.zh: 王莽遣平均公廉丹、太師王匡擊之。

translations.literal: Wang Mang sent Equalizing Duke Lian Dan, Grand Tutor Wang Kuang to strike them.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang sent Lian Dan and Wang Kuang against them.

sentences.zh: 崇等欲戰，恐其眾與莽兵亂，乃皆朱其眉以相識別，由是號曰赤眉。

translations.literal: Chong and others wished to fight, feared their crowd with Mang’s troops would mix in confusion, then all reddened their eyebrows to recognize one another; from this were called Red Eyebrows.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing their men would mingle with government troops in battle, they dyed their eyebrows red for recognition—hence the name Red Eyebrows.

sentences.zh: 赤眉遂大破丹、匡軍，殺萬餘人，追至無鹽，廉丹戰死，王匡走。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows then greatly broke Dan’s and Kuang’s army, killed over ten thousand, pursued to Wuyan; Lian Dan died in battle, Wang Kuang fled.
translations.idiomatic: They shattered Lian Dan and Wang Kuang, killed ten thousand, pursued them to Wuyan, where Lian Dan fell and Wang Kuang escaped.

sentences.zh: 崇又引其兵十餘萬，復還圍莒，數月。

translations.literal: Chong again led his troops over a hundred thousand, again returned to besiege Ju, several months.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong brought more than a hundred thousand men back to besiege Ju for months.

sentences.zh: 或說崇曰：『莒，父母之國，奈何攻之？

translations.literal: Someone persuaded Chong, saying, ‘Ju is the state of your fathers and mothers—how can you attack it?
translations.idiomatic: Someone urged him that Ju was their homeland and should not be attacked.

sentences.zh: 』乃解去。

translations.literal: ’ Then they broke the siege and left.
translations.idiomatic: He lifted the siege.

sentences.zh: 時呂母病死，其眾分入赤眉、青犢、銅馬中。

translations.literal: At the time Mother Lü died of illness; her crowd divided and entered among the Red Eyebrows, Green Calves, and Bronze Horse.
translations.idiomatic: When Mother Lü died her followers split among the Red Eyebrows, Green Calves, and Bronze Horse bands.

sentences.zh: 赤眉遂寇東海，與王莽沂平大尹戰，敗，死者數千人，乃引去，掠楚、沛、汝南、潁川，還人陳留，攻拔魯城，轉至濮陽。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows then raided Donghai, battled with Wang Mang’s Yiping Grand Intendant, were defeated, several thousand dead; then led away, plundered Chu, Pei, Runan, Yingchuan, returned entered Chenliu, attacked and took Lu city, turned to Puyang.
translations.idiomatic: They raided Donghai, lost thousands to Wang Mang’s local commander, withdrew, plundered through Chu, Pei, Runan, and Yingchuan, doubled back through Chenliu, took Lu, and moved on to Puyang.

sentences.zh: 會更始都洛陽，遣使降崇。

translations.literal: It happened that Gengshi made capital at Luoyang, sent envoys to make Chong surrender.
translations.idiomatic: When Gengshi established his capital at Luoyang he sent envoys to accept Fan Chong’s submission.

sentences.zh: 崇等聞漢室復興，即留其兵，自將渠帥二十餘人，隨使者至洛陽降更始，皆封為列侯。

translations.literal: Chong and others heard the Han house had revived, then left their troops, himself led over twenty band chiefs, followed the envoy to Luoyang to surrender to Gengshi; all were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that Han had revived, Fan Chong left his army in camp and brought more than twenty chiefs to Luoyang to surrender; all were enfeoffed as marquises.

sentences.zh: 崇等既未有國邑，而留眾稍有離叛，乃遂亡歸其營，將兵入潁川，分其眾為二部，崇與逄安為一部，徐宣、謝祿、楊音為一部。

translations.literal: Chong and others since they had not yet received state lands, and the left-behind crowd gradually had some desertion and rebellion, then fled back to their camp, led troops into Yingchuan, divided their crowd into two parts: Chong together with Pang An as one part, Xu Xuan, Xie Lu, and Yang Yin as one part.
translations.idiomatic: They had received no fiefs, and their men in camp began to drift away, so they fled back to their army, marched into Yingchuan, and split into two columns under Fan Chong and Pang An on one side and Xu Xuan, Xie Lu, and Yang Yin on the other.

sentences.zh: 崇、安攻拔長社，南擊宛，斬縣令；

translations.literal: Chong and An attacked and took Changshe, struck south Wan, beheaded the county magistrate;
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong and Pang An took Changshe, struck south to Wan, and beheaded the magistrate;

sentences.zh: 而宣、祿等亦拔陽翟，引之梁，擊殺河南太守。

translations.literal: while Xuan, Lu, and others also took Yangzhai, led them to Liang, struck and killed the Administrator of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: while Xu Xuan and Xie Lu took Yangzhai, advanced into Liang, and killed the governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 赤眉眾雖數戰勝，而疲敝厭兵，皆日夜愁泣，思欲東歸。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrow host although repeatedly victorious in battle, yet was exhausted and weary of arms; all day and night sorrowfully wept, thinking to wish east to return.
translations.idiomatic: Though often victorious, the men were worn out and homesick, weeping day and night to go home to the east.

sentences.zh: 崇等計議，慮眾東向必散，不如西攻長安。

translations.literal: Chong and others deliberated, feared if the crowd went east they would surely scatter; better west to attack Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong reasoned that a march east would dissolve the host, whereas a drive on Chang’an might hold them together.

sentences.zh: 更始二年冬，崇、安自武關，宣等從陸渾關，兩道俱入。

translations.literal: In the second year of Gengshi’s winter, Chong and An from Wu Pass, Xuan and others from Luhun Pass, two routes together entered.
translations.idiomatic: In the winter of the second year of Gengshi they invaded in two columns, Fan Chong and Pang An through Wu Pass and Xu Xuan’s group through Luhun Pass.

sentences.zh: 三年正月，俱至弘農，與更始諸將連戰克勝，眾遂大集。

translations.literal: In the third year’s first month, both reached Hongnong, with Gengshi’s various generals battled connectedly and prevailed; the crowd then greatly gathered.
translations.idiomatic: They joined at Hongnong in the first month of the third year, defeated Gengshi’s generals in a series of fights, and their strength swelled.

sentences.zh: 乃分萬人為一營，凡三十營，營置三老、從事各一人。

translations.literal: Then they divided ten thousand men as one camp, in all thirty camps; each camp set one Elder and one Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: They organized thirty camps of ten thousand men each, every camp headed by an elder and a clerk.

sentences.zh: 進至華陰。

translations.literal: They advanced to Huayin.
translations.idiomatic: They advanced to Huayin.

sentences.zh: 初，赤眉過式，掠盆子及二兄恭、茂，皆在軍中。

translations.literal: At first, when the Red Eyebrows passed Shi, they plundered Penzi and his two elder brothers Gong and Mao; all were in the army.
translations.idiomatic: When the Red Eyebrows passed through Shi they carried off Liu Penzi and his brothers Liu Gong and Liu Mao into the ranks.

sentences.zh: 恭少習《尚書》，略通大義。

translations.literal: Gong in youth studied the Documents, broadly grasped the great meanings.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong had studied the Book of Documents in youth and knew its main themes.

sentences.zh: 及隨崇等降更始，即封為式侯。

translations.literal: When he followed Chong and others to surrender Gengshi, at once was enfeoffed Marquis of Shi.
translations.idiomatic: When he surrendered with Fan Chong he was enfeoffed marquis of Shi.

sentences.zh: 以明經數言事，拜侍中，從更始在長安。

translations.literal: Because of clarifying the classics repeatedly speaking on affairs, was appointed Palace Attendant, followed Gengshi at Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: His classical learning won him repeated audiences; he was made palace attendant and accompanied Gengshi at Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 盆子與茂留軍中，屬右校卒史劉俠卿，主芻牧牛，號曰牛吏。

translations.literal: Penzi and Mao remained in the army, belonged to Right Camp Sergeant Liu Xiaqing, in charge of forage herding cattle, called Ox Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Penzi and Liu Mao stayed in camp under Sergeant Liu Xiaqing as herd boys, nicknamed the ox clerks.

sentences.zh: 及崇等欲立帝，求軍中景王後者，得七十餘人，唯盆子與茂及前西安侯劉孝最為近屬。

translations.literal: When Chong and others wished to establish an emperor, they sought in the army descendants of Prince Jing, obtained over seventy persons; only Penzi and Mao and former Marquis of Xi’an Liu Xiao were closest kin.
translations.idiomatic: When Fan Chong sought a descendant of Prince Jing of Chengyang among the soldiers he found more than seventy, but only Liu Penzi, Liu Mao, and the former marquis of Xi’an, Liu Xiao, were close agnates.

sentences.zh: 崇等議曰：『聞古天子將兵稱上將軍。

translations.literal: Chong and others deliberated, saying, ‘We have heard that in antiquity the Son of Heaven who led troops called himself supreme general.
translations.idiomatic: They agreed that ancient emperors who took the field had styled themselves supreme general.

sentences.zh: 』乃書劄為符曰『上將軍』，又以兩空劄置笥中，遂於鄭北設壇場，祠城陽景王。

translations.literal: ’ Then they wrote slips as talismans saying ‘Supreme General,’ again placed two empty slips in a box; then north of Zheng set up an altar field, sacrificed to Prince Jing of Chengyang.
translations.idiomatic: They wrote “supreme general” on one lot and left two blanks, sealed them in a box, built an altar north of Zheng, and sacrificed to Prince Zhang of Chengyang.

sentences.zh: 諸三老、從事皆大會陛下，列盆子等三人居中立，以年次探劄。

translations.literal: The various elders and clerks all assembled in great conclave, arrayed Penzi and three others standing in the center, by age order drew slips.
translations.idiomatic: The elders and clerks assembled at the site, stood Penzi and the other two candidates in the center, and had them draw lots in order of age.

sentences.zh: 盆子最幼，後探得符，諸將乃皆稱臣拜。

translations.literal: Penzi was youngest; afterward he drew and obtained the tally; the generals then all called themselves ministers and bowed.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi, the youngest, drew last—and pulled the marked lot; the chiefs hailed him as emperor and bowed.

sentences.zh: 盆子時年十五，被髮徒跣，敝衣赭汗，見眾拜，恐畏欲啼。

translations.literal: Penzi at the time was fifteen, hair loose barefoot, tattered clothes red with sweat; seeing the crowd bow, fear and dread wished to weep.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen years old, disheveled and barefoot in rags stained with sweat, he faced the kneeling host in terror and nearly wept.

sentences.zh: 茂謂曰：『善藏符。

translations.literal: Mao said to him, ‘Well hide the tally.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Mao whispered to hide the lot safely.

sentences.zh: 』盆子即嚙折棄之，復還依俠卿。

translations.literal: ’ Penzi at once bit, broke, and discarded it, again returned to rely on Xiaqing.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi bit the slip in two, threw it away, and fled back to Liu Xiaqing’s herd camp.

sentences.zh: 俠卿為制絳單衣、半頭赤幘、直綦履，乘軒車大馬，赤屏泥，絳襜絡，而猶從牧兒遨。

translations.literal: Xiaqing for him made crimson unlined garment, half-head red turban, straight-qi shoes, rode screened carriage big horse, red screen mud, crimson chan-strap net, yet still followed herd boys in roaming.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiaqing dressed him in crimson court dress, a red kerchief, straw sandals, a carriage with red mudguards and crimson trappings—yet he still roamed with the herd boys.

sentences.zh: 崇雖起勇力而為眾所宗，然不知書數。

translations.literal: Although Chong rose from courage and strength and was honored by the crowd, yet he did not know writing and reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong was a fighting man whom the host followed but he could neither read nor reckon accounts.

sentences.zh: 徐宣故縣獄吏，能通《易經》。

translations.literal: Xu Xuan was formerly a county jail clerk, able to penetrate the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Xuan had been a county jailer and understood the Book of Changes.

sentences.zh: 遂共推宣為丞相、崇御史大夫、逄安左大司馬、謝祿右大司馬，自楊音以下皆為列卿。

translations.literal: Then together they pushed Xuan as Chancellor, Chong Imperial Counsellor, Pang An Left Grand Marshal, Xie Lu Right Grand Marshal; from Yang Yin downward all became column ministers.
translations.idiomatic: They made Xu Xuan chancellor, Fan Chong imperial counsellor, Pang An and Xie Lu left and right grand marshals, and Yang Yin and the rest senior ministers.

sentences.zh: 軍及高陵，與更始叛將張卬等連和，遂攻東都門，入長安城，更始來降。

translations.literal: The army reached Gaoling, joined with Gengshi’s rebel general Zhang Ang and others in alliance, then attacked the Eastern Capital Gate, entered Chang’an city, Gengshi came to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: They reached Gaoling, allied with Zhang Ang and other mutinous officers, stormed the eastern capital gate, took Chang’an, and received Gengshi’s surrender.

sentences.zh: 盆子居長東宮，諸將日會論功，爭言讙呼，拔劍擊柱，不能相一。

translations.literal: Penzi dwelt at the Long East Palace; the generals daily met to debate merit, contended in speech with clamor, drew swords and struck pillars, could not unify.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi lived in the Changle East Palace while the chiefs met daily to wrangle over rewards, shouting and striking pillars with their swords, unable to agree.

sentences.zh: 三輔郡縣營長遣使貢獻，兵士輒剽奪之。

translations.literal: The Three Assistants’ prefectures and counties’ camp chiefs sent envoys with tribute; the soldiers then plundered it.
translations.idiomatic: When local leaders across the Three Assistants sent tribute, the troops seized it before it ever reached the court.

sentences.zh: 又數虜暴吏民，百姓保壁，由是皆復固守。

translations.literal: Moreover repeatedly they seized and ravaged clerks and commoners; the hundred surnames guarded their ramparts; thereby all again firmly defended.
translations.idiomatic: They repeatedly raided officials and townsfolk, so the people fled behind stockades and everywhere dug in again.

sentences.zh: 至臘日，崇等乃設樂大會，盆子坐正殿，中黃門持兵在後，公卿皆列坐殿上。

translations.literal: When it reached the la-day, Chong and others then set up music and held a great assembly; Penzi sat in the main hall; palace attendants behind held weapons; dukes and ministers all sat in rows on the hall.
translations.idiomatic: On the year-end la festival Fan Chong staged a banquet with music. Penzi took the throne while armed eunuchs stood behind him and the whole court sat ranked on the dais.

sentences.zh: 酒未行，其中一人也刀筆書謁欲賀，其餘不知書者請起之，各各屯聚，更相背向。

translations.literal: The wine had not yet circulated; among them one person with knife-brush wrote a petition wishing to congratulate; the rest not knowing writing requested him to rise for it; each each gathered in knots, mutually turned backs.
translations.idiomatic: Before the wine had made a round, one man drafted a congratulatory memorial with his clerk’s brush; those who could not write pressed him to stand and read it, while factions huddled apart and glared past one another’s shoulders.

sentences.zh: 大司農楊音按劍罵曰：『諸卿皆老傭也！

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Agriculture Yang Yin grasped his sword and cursed, saying, “You gentlemen are all old hired hands!
translations.idiomatic: Grand Minister of Agriculture Yang Yin gripped his sword and cursed, “You lot are nothing but hired rustics!

sentences.zh: 今日設君臣之禮，反更殽亂，兒戲尚不如此，皆可格殺！

translations.literal: Today having set lord-minister ritual, contrary moreover is confusion; children’s play is not yet thus; all may be struck and killed!
translations.idiomatic: Today we arranged the rites of ruler and minister, yet you have made a worse shambles than a children’s game—every one of you deserves summary execution!”

sentences.zh: 』更相辯鬥，而兵眾遂各逾宮斬關，入掠酒肉，互相殺傷。

translations.literal: ” They mutually disputed and fought, and the troops then each crossed the palace and cut the passes, entered plundering wine and meat, mutually wounded and killed.
translations.idiomatic: They hurled abuse and blows at one another, bands of soldiers swarmed over palace walls and through the gates, looted wine and meat, and hacked one another down in the corridors.

sentences.zh: 衛尉諸葛稚聞之，勒兵人，格殺百餘人，乃定。

translations.literal: Commandant of the Guards Zhuge Zhi heard it, led troops in, struck and killed more than a hundred persons, then it was settled.
translations.idiomatic: Commandant of the Guards Zhuge Zhi heard the uproar, led his guard in, cut down more than a hundred rioters, and restored order.

sentences.zh: 盆子惶恐，日夜啼泣，獨與中黃門共臥起，唯得上觀閣而不聞外事。

translations.literal: Penzi was frightened, day and night wept; alone with palace attendants together lay and rose; only able to ascend the observation tower and not hear outer affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi was terrified and wept day and night; he slept and woke only with a few eunuchs and could climb the belvedere but heard nothing of what happened outside.

sentences.zh: 時掖庭中宮女猶有數百千人，自更始敗後，幽閉殿內，掘庭中蘆菔根，捕池魚而食之，死者因相埋於宮中。

translations.literal: At that time in the Rear Palace palace ladies still were several hundred to a thousand persons; since Gengshi’s defeat, shut in the halls, dug courtyard turnip roots, caught pond fish and ate them; the dead therefore mutually buried in the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Hundreds of palace women were still shut in the rear halls after Gengshi’s fall; they dug turnips from the courtyards, caught fish from the pools, and when one died the rest buried her inside the compound.

sentences.zh: 有故祠甘泉樂人，尚共擊鼓歌舞，衣服鮮明，見盆子叩頭言饑。

translations.literal: There were former shrine Ganquan musicians, still together beat drums and sang and danced, garments bright; seeing Penzi knocked head and spoke hunger.
translations.idiomatic: Some former musicians of the Ganquan shrine still drummed and danced in bright costumes; when they saw Penzi they kowtowed and begged for food.

sentences.zh: 盆子使中黃門稟之米，人數斗。

translations.literal: Penzi ordered palace attendants report to them grain, each several pecks.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi sent eunuchs to give them a few pecks of grain each.

sentences.zh: 後盆子去，皆餓死不出。

translations.literal: After Penzi departed, all starved dead and did not come out.
translations.idiomatic: After Penzi left the capital, none of them ever emerged; they starved inside the palace.

sentences.zh: 劉恭見赤眉眾亂，知其必敗，自恐兄弟俱禍，密教盆子歸璽綬，習為辭讓之言。

translations.literal: Liu Gong saw the Red Eyebrows’ host in disorder, knew they must be defeated, himself feared brothers together calamity, secretly taught Penzi to return seal-cord, practiced making declining words.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong saw the Red Eyebrows sliding into chaos and knew they would fall; fearing for his own kin, he secretly coached Penzi to surrender the imperial seals and rehearse a speech of abdication.

sentences.zh: 建武二年正朔，崇等大會，劉恭先曰：『諸君共立恭弟為帝，德誠深厚。

translations.literal: Jianwu second year first month, Chong and others great assembly, Liu Gong first said, “You gentlemen together set up Gong’s younger brother as emperor; virtue is truly deep and thick.
translations.idiomatic: At the new year of Jianwu 2 Fan Chong called a great council. Liu Gong rose first and said, “You raised my younger brother to the throne; your kindness has been profound.

sentences.zh: 立且一年，肴亂日甚，誠不足以相成。

translations.literal: Established nearly one year, confusion daily more severe, truly not sufficient to assist completion.
translations.idiomatic: A year has passed and disorder grows worse by the day—we are no fit support for him.

sentences.zh: 恐死而無所益，願得退為庶人，更求賢知，唯諸君省察。

translations.literal: Fearing death yet no benefit, wish to obtain retreat as commoner, again seek worthy wise, only you gentlemen examine.
translations.idiomatic: If he dies it will profit no one. Let him step down as a commoner while you seek a worthier man—I beg you to consider it.”

sentences.zh: 』崇等謝曰：『此皆崇等罪也。

translations.literal: ” Chong and others apologized saying, “This is all Chong and others’ crime.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong and the others bowed and said, “The fault is ours alone.

sentences.zh: 』恭復固請。

translations.literal: ” Gong again firmly requested.
translations.idiomatic: ” Liu Gong pressed his plea again.

sentences.zh: 或曰：『此寧式侯事邪！

translations.literal: Someone said, “Is this the Marquis of Ningshi’s affair!
translations.idiomatic: Someone snapped, “Since when is that the Marquis of Ningshi’s business!”

sentences.zh: 』恭惶恐起去。

translations.literal: ” Gong frightened rose and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong paled, rose, and withdrew in alarm.

sentences.zh: 盆子乃下床解璽綬，叩頭曰：『今設置縣官而為賊如故。

translations.literal: Penzi then descended the couch undid seal-cord, knocked head saying, “Now having set up county officials yet acting as bandits as before.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi stepped from the dais, unfastened the seals and cords, and kowtowed. “We have installed a government, yet we still behave like robbers.

sentences.zh: 吏人貢獻，輒見剽劫，流聞四方，莫不怨恨，不復信向。

translations.literal: Clerks and people tribute, then seen plundered; flowing heard four quarters, none not resentful hate, no longer trust toward.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever officials brought tribute your men seized it; word spread to every corner of the realm, and no one trusts or looks to us any longer.

sentences.zh: 此皆立非其人所致，願乞骸骨，避賢聖。

translations.literal: This is all from enthroning not its person caused; wish to beg bones, yield to worthy sage.
translations.idiomatic: This is what comes of crowning the wrong man. I beg leave to resign my bones and yield the throne to the wise.”

sentences.zh: 必欲殺盆子以塞責者，無所離死。

translations.literal: If must wish kill Penzi to stop blame, nowhere escape death.
translations.idiomatic: If you mean to kill me to answer for it, I cannot flee death anyway.

sentences.zh: 誠冀諸君肯哀憐之耳！

translations.literal: Truly hope you gentlemen willing pity me!
translations.idiomatic: I only hope you will pity me instead!”

sentences.zh: 』因涕泣噓唏。

translations.literal: ” Then weeping tears sob-sigh.
translations.idiomatic: He broke down sobbing and could not catch his breath.

sentences.zh: 崇等及會者數百人，莫不哀憐之，乃皆避席頓首曰：『臣無狀，負陛下。

translations.literal: Chong and others and assembly persons several hundred persons, none not pitied him, then all left mat knocked head saying, “We lack deportment, failed Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong and the hundreds at the meeting were moved to pity; they left their mats and kowtowed. “We have behaved shamefully and failed Your Majesty.

sentences.zh: 請自今已後，不敢復放縱。

translations.literal: Request from now after, not dare again loose unrestrained.
translations.idiomatic: From this day we dare not run wild again.”

sentences.zh: 』因共抱持盆子，帶以璽綬。

translations.literal: Then together they embraced and held Penzi, belted him with the seal-cord.
translations.idiomatic: They crowded around Penzi, lifted him up, and fastened the seals on him again.

sentences.zh: 盆子號呼不得已。

translations.literal: Penzi cried out had no choice.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi wailed and struggled but could not refuse.

sentences.zh: 既罷出，各閉營自守，三輔翕然，稱天子聰明。

translations.literal: When finished went out, each closed camp self-defended; Three Assistants uniformly, called the Son of Heaven wise bright.
translations.idiomatic: When the meeting broke up each chief sealed his camp. Across the Three Assistants people said in one voice that the emperor was wise.

sentences.zh: 百姓爭還長安，市裏且滿。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames vied returning to Chang’an; market wards nearly full.
translations.idiomatic: Common folk rushed back to Chang’an until the wards were crowded again.

sentences.zh: 後二十餘日，赤眉貪財物，復出大掠。

translations.literal: After more than twenty days, the Red Eyebrows coveted goods and wealth, again went out in great plunder.
translations.idiomatic: Some twenty days later the Red Eyebrows’ greed for loot drove them out to pillage the capital once more.

sentences.zh: 城中糧食盡，遂收載珍寶，因大縱火燒宮室，引兵而西。

translations.literal: Inside the city grain food exhausted, then gathered loaded treasures, thereby greatly set fire burned palace chambers, led troops and went west.
translations.idiomatic: When the granaries were empty they loaded treasure onto carts, torched the palaces, and marched the army westward.

sentences.zh: 過祠南郊，車甲兵馬最為猛盛，眾號百萬。

translations.literal: Passing sacrificed at Southern Suburb, chariots armor troops horses most fierce strong; the host called itself a million.
translations.idiomatic: They sacrificed at the southern suburban altar; chariots, mail, and horse columns stretched farther than ever, and they boasted a million men.

sentences.zh: 盆子乘王車，駕三馬，從數百騎。

translations.literal: Penzi rode king’s carriage, harnessed three horses, followed by several hundred riders.
translations.idiomatic: Penzi rode in the imperial coach behind a three-horse team with hundreds of horsemen at his heels.

sentences.zh: 乃自南山轉掠城邑，與更始將軍嚴春戰於郿，破春，殺之，遂入安定、北地。

translations.literal: Then from South Mountain turned plundering cities, with Gengshi’s general Yan Chun fought at Mei, defeated Chun, killed him, then entered Anding and Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Swinging down from the southern hills they raided towns, defeated and killed Gengshi’s general Yan Chun at Mei, then pushed into Anding and Beidi.

sentences.zh: 至陽城、番須中，逢大雪，坑谷皆滿，士多凍死，乃復還，發掘諸陵，取其寶貨，遂汙辱呂後屍，凡賊所發，有玉匣殮者率皆如生，故赤眉得多行淫穢。

translations.literal: Reaching Yangcheng and Fanxu middle, met great snow, pits and valleys all filled, soldiers many froze dead, then again returned, dug up the various tombs, took their treasures, then defiled Empress Lü’s corpse; wherever the bandits opened, those with jade casket encoffining mostly all like living; therefore the Red Eyebrows could much commit lewd filth.
translations.idiomatic: Caught in a blizzard between Yangcheng and Fanxu, they filled ravines with frozen corpses, turned back, and opened the imperial tombs for their gold. They violated the body of Empress Lü; wherever robbers pried open a jade burial suit the corpse looked still alive, so the Red Eyebrows committed every outrage.

sentences.zh: 大司徒鄧禹時在長安，遣兵擊之於郁夷，反為所敗，禹乃出之雲陽。

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Education Deng Yu at that time was in Chang’an, sent troops struck them at Yuyi, contrary was by them defeated, Yu then went out to Yunyang.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Minister Deng Yu was still in Chang’an; he sent troops against them at Yuyi but was beaten and withdrew to Yunyang.

sentences.zh: 九月，赤眉復入長安，止桂宮。

translations.literal: Ninth month, the Red Eyebrows again entered Chang’an, halted at Cassia Palace.
translations.idiomatic: In the ninth month the Red Eyebrows reoccupied Chang’an and camped in the Cassia Palace.

sentences.zh: 時，漢中賊延岑出散關，屯杜陵，逄安將十餘萬人擊之。

translations.literal: At that time, Hanzhong bandit Yan Cen exited Sanguan, encamped at Duling, Pang An led more than a hundred thousand men struck him.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the Hanzhong rebel Yan Cen came through Sanguan and camped at Duling; Pang An marched well over a hundred thousand men against him.

sentences.zh: 鄧禹以逄安精兵在外，唯盆子與羸弱居城中，乃自往攻之。

translations.literal: Deng Yu because Pang An’s crack troops were outside, only Penzi and weak dwelt in the city, then himself went to attack them.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Yu judged that Pang An’s veterans were away and only Penzi and a garrison of invalids remained, so he attacked the city himself.

sentences.zh: 會謝祿救至，夜戰槀街中，禹兵敗走。

translations.literal: As Xie Lu’s relief arrived, night battle in Gaojie lane, Yu’s troops defeated fled.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Lu’s relief column arrived and a night fight erupted in Gaojie; Deng Yu’s army broke and fled.

sentences.zh: 延岑及更始將軍李寶合兵數萬人，與逄安戰於杜陵。

translations.literal: Yan Cen and Gengshi’s general Li Bao joined troops several tens of thousands of men, with Pang An fought at Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Cen and Gengshi’s general Li Bao united tens of thousands of men and offered battle to Pang An at Duling.

sentences.zh: 岑等大敗，死者萬餘人，寶遂降安，而延岑收散卒走。

translations.literal: Cen and others greatly defeated, dead more than ten thousand persons, Bao then surrendered to An, while Yan Cen gathered scattered troops fled.
