translations.idiomatic: Yan Cen was routed with over ten thousand dead; Li Bao went over to Pang An, while Yan Cen rallied the survivors and slipped away.

sentences.zh: 寶乃密使人謂岑曰：『子努力還戰，吾當於內反之，表裏合勢，可大破也。

translations.literal: Bao then secretly sent person tell Cen saying, “You strive return fight; I shall within turn against them; outer inner join force, can greatly break them.
translations.idiomatic: Li Bao secretly sent word to Yan Cen: “Fight your way back in earnest; I will turn the camp from within. Together we can crush them.”

sentences.zh: 』岑即還挑戰，安等空營擊之，寶從後悉拔赤眉旌幟，更立己幡旗。

translations.literal: Cen then returned challenging; An and others emptied camp to strike them; Bao from behind all pulled up Red Eyebrows’ banners, again set his own pennant flags.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Cen wheeled about and offered battle; Pang An led the host out to meet him while Li Bao tore down every Red Eyebrows standard from the rear and raised his own.

sentences.zh: 安等戰疲還營，見旗幟皆白，大驚亂走，自投川谷，死者十餘萬，逄安與數千人脫歸長安。

translations.literal: An and others war weary returned camp, saw banners all white, greatly frightened chaotic fled, self threw into streams and valleys, dead more than a hundred thousand, Pang An with several thousand men escaped returned Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Weary from battle, Pang An’s men saw unfamiliar white banners over their tents, panicked, and stampeded into gorges—more than a hundred thousand died. Pang An escaped to Chang’an with only a few thousand.

sentences.zh: 時三輔大亂，人相食，城郭皆空，白骨蔽野，遺人往往聚為營保，各堅守不下。

translations.literal: At that time Three Assistants great chaos, people mutually ate, walls all empty, white bones cover wild; survivors often gathered as camp forts, each firmly defended not yielding.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Assistants descended into chaos: men ate one another, towns stood empty, and bones whitened the fields. Survivors clustered in stockades and refused every summons.

sentences.zh: 赤眉虜掠無所得，十二月，乃引而東歸，眾尚二十餘萬，隨道復散。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows plundered seized nothing gained, twelfth month, then led and east returned; the host still more than two hundred thousand, along the road again scattered.
translations.idiomatic: Unable to squeeze more loot from the land, in the twelfth month they turned eastward. Two hundred thousand still marched, but the column melted away along the road.

sentences.zh: 光武乃遣破奸將軍侯進等屯新安，建威大將軍耿弇等屯宜陽，分為二道，以要其還路。

translations.literal: Guangwu then sent Bandit-Smashing General Hou Jin and others encamp at Xin’an, Establishing Might Grand General Geng Yan and others encamp at Yiyang, divided into two roads, to intercept their return route.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu posted Bandit-Smashing General Hou Jin at Xin’an and Establishing Might Grand General Geng Yan at Yiyang in two cordons to cut off their retreat.

sentences.zh: 敕諸將曰：『賊若東走，可引宜陽兵會新安；

translations.literal: Ordered the generals saying, “If the bandits east flee, can lead Yiyang troops meet Xin’an;
translations.idiomatic: He instructed his commanders: “If the bandits strike east, bring the Yiyang column to join Xin’an;

sentences.zh: 賊若南走，可引新安兵會宜陽。

translations.literal: if the bandits south flee, can lead Xin’an troops meet Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: if they veer south, march the Xin’an troops to Yiyang.”

sentences.zh: 』明年正月，鄧禹自河北度，擊赤眉於湖，禹復敗走，赤眉遂出關南向。

translations.literal: Next year first month, Deng Yu from Hebei crossed, struck the Red Eyebrows at Hu, Yu again defeated fled; the Red Eyebrows then exited the pass southward.
translations.idiomatic: The next first month Deng Yu crossed from the north and attacked the Red Eyebrows at Hu but was routed again; the rebels broke out of the pass and drove south.

sentences.zh: 征西大將軍馮異破之於崤底。

translations.literal: Campaign West Grand General Feng Yi defeated them at Yaodi.
translations.idiomatic: Campaign West Grand General Feng Yi shattered them at the foot of the Xiao Mountains.

sentences.zh: 帝聞，乃自將幸宜陽，盛兵以邀其走路。

translations.literal: The Emperor heard, then personally led visited Yiyang, massed troops to intercept their fleeing route.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor heard the news he led the host in person to Yiyang and massed an army across their line of retreat.

sentences.zh: 赤眉忽遇大軍，驚震不知所為，乃遣劉恭乞降，曰：『盆子將百萬眾降，陛下何以待之？

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrows suddenly met great army, alarmed shaken knew not what to do, then sent Liu Gong beg surrender, saying, “Penzi leads a million host to surrender; Your Majesty by what treat them?
translations.idiomatic: The Red Eyebrows blundered into the imperial host, froze in terror, and sent Liu Gong to sue for peace. “Penzi brings a million men to yield,” he asked. “How will Your Majesty treat us?”

sentences.zh: 』帝曰：『待汝以不死耳。

translations.literal: The Emperor said, “I grant you life—that is all.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered, “You have my word: I will spare your lives.”

sentences.zh: 』樊崇乃將盆子及丞相徐宣以下三十餘人肉袒降。

translations.literal: Fan Chong then led Penzi and Chancellor Xu Xuan and below more than thirty persons bare-breasted to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong then brought Penzi, Chancellor Xu Xuan, and more than thirty followers, stripped to the waist, to surrender.

sentences.zh: 上所得傳國璽綬，更始七尺寶斂及玉璧各一。

translations.literal: They presented the recovered Heirloom Seal of the Realm and its cords, one seven-chi treasure shroud of Gengshi’s, and one jade bi.
translations.idiomatic: They surrendered the Heirloom Seal with its cords, Gengshi’s seven-foot imperial burial shroud, and a single ceremonial jade disc.

sentences.zh: 積兵甲宜陽城西，與熊耳山齊。

translations.literal: Piled weapons and armor west of Yiyang city, level with Bear Ear Mountain.
translations.idiomatic: Captured arms were heaped west of Yiyang until the pile rose level with Bear Ear Mountain.

sentences.zh: 帝令縣廚賜食，眾積困餧，十餘萬人皆得飽飫。

translations.literal: The Emperor ordered the county kitchen grant food; the masses long famished, more than a hundred thousand persons all obtained full satiety.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told the county kitchens to feed them; well over a hundred thousand half-starved men ate their fill at last.

sentences.zh: 明旦，大陳兵馬臨洛水，令盆子君臣列而觀之。

translations.literal: Next dawn, greatly arrayed troops and horses overlooking the Luo River, ordered Penzi ruler-minister to line and watch them.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn the next day he drew up horse and foot along the Luo and made Penzi and his ministers stand in ranks to watch.

sentences.zh: 謂盆子曰：『自知當死不？

translations.literal: Said to Penzi, “Self know ought die or not?
translations.idiomatic: He asked Penzi, “Do you know you deserve death?”

sentences.zh: 』對曰：『罪當應死，猶幸上憐赦之耳。

translations.literal: ” Answered saying, “Crime ought answer death, still fortunate the sovereign pities and pardons him only.”
translations.idiomatic: Penzi replied, “My guilt merits death, yet I hope Your Majesty will pity and spare me.”

sentences.zh: 』帝笑曰：『兒大黠，宗室無蚩者。

translations.literal: ” The Emperor laughed saying, “The lad greatly clever; the imperial clan has no dull ones.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor laughed. “Sharp boy—we have no dullards left in the imperial house.”

sentences.zh: 』又謂崇等曰：『得無悔降乎？

translations.literal: ” Again said to Chong and others, “Perhaps regret having surrendered?
translations.idiomatic: Turning to Fan Chong and the rest he asked, “Do you regret yielding?”

sentences.zh: 朕今遣卿歸營勒兵，鳴鼓相攻，決其勝負，不欲強相服也。

translations.literal: I now send you return camp to marshal troops, beat drums mutually attack, decide their victory and defeat; I do not wish forcibly to make you submit.”
translations.idiomatic: I could send you back to camp, beat the drums, and let you fight it out—I need not force your submission.”

sentences.zh: 』徐宣等叩頭曰：『臣等出長安東都門，君臣計議，歸命聖德。

translations.literal: Xu Xuan and others knocked heads saying, “We and others exited Chang’an’s Eastern Capital Gate, ruler and ministers planned counsel, submitted fate to sagely virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Xuan and his companions kowtowed. “When we left the eastern capital gate of Chang’an, lord and ministers had already resolved to throw ourselves on Your Majesty’s grace.”

sentences.zh: 百姓可與樂成，難與圖始，故不告眾耳。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames can with rejoice in completion, hard with plan the beginning; therefore we did not tell the masses only.”
translations.idiomatic: The common people will share a triumph but not a risky beginning, so we dared not announce it to the ranks.”

sentences.zh: 今日得降，猶去虎口歸慈母，誠歡誠喜，無所恨也。

translations.literal: Today obtaining surrender, still like leaving tiger mouth returning loving mother; truly glad truly joyful, nothing to regret.”
translations.idiomatic: To surrender today is like stepping from a tiger’s jaws into a mother’s arms—we are glad beyond measure and harbor no regret.”

sentences.zh: 帝曰：『卿所謂鐵中錚錚，庸中佼佼者也。

translations.literal: The Emperor said, “This is what is meant by ‘true steel among iron, a standout among the mediocre.’”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, “You are what men mean by the true steel among iron and the brightest spark among the common run.”

sentences.zh: 』又曰：『諸卿大為無道，所過皆夷滅老弱，溺社稷，汙井竈。

translations.literal: Again he said, “You gentlemen greatly acted without the Way; wherever you passed all exterminated old and weak, drowned the altars of soil and grain, defiled wells and stoves.
translations.idiomatic: He went on: “You have behaved monstrously—wherever you marched you slaughtered the helpless, cast down the state altars, and fouled hearth and well.”

sentences.zh: 然猶有三善：攻破城邑，周遍天下，本故妻婦無所改易，是一善也；

translations.literal: Yet still there are three goods: attacking and breaking cities, roaming the whole realm, original wives changed nothing—that is one good;
translations.idiomatic: Yet you have three redeeming deeds: though you stormed towns across the empire, you never put aside the wives you married before—that is the first.”

sentences.zh: 立君能用宗室，是二善也；

translations.literal: Raising a ruler able to use imperial clan—that is two goods;
translations.idiomatic: You raised a sovereign from the imperial bloodline—the second.”

sentences.zh: 餘賊立君，迫急皆持其首降，自以為功，諸卿獨完全以付朕，是三善也。

translations.literal: Other bandits raising rulers, pressed urgent all held their heads to surrender, deeming it their merit; you gentlemen alone wholly intact handed them to Us—that is three goods.
translations.idiomatic: Other rebels, when cornered, chop off their puppet’s head and claim the credit; you alone delivered your emperor whole—the third.”

sentences.zh: 』乃令各與妻子居洛陽，賜宅人一區，田二頃。

translations.literal: Then he ordered each with wife and children to dwell at Luoyang, granted a house one compound, fields two qing.
translations.idiomatic: He bade them settle in Luoyang with their families, granting each a house and two qing of land.

sentences.zh: 其夏，樊崇、逄安謀反，誅死。

translations.literal: That summer, Fan Chong and Pang An plotted rebellion, were executed and died.
translations.idiomatic: That summer Fan Chong and Pang An plotted revolt and were put to death.

sentences.zh: 楊音在長安時，遇趙王良有恩，賜爵關內侯，與徐宣俱歸鄉里，卒於家。

translations.literal: Yang Yin when in Chang’an met Prince of Zhao Liu Liang with kindness, was granted rank marquis within the passes; together with Xu Xuan returned to home village, died at home.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Yin had once done a kindness to Prince Liu Liang of Zhao and was ennobled as a marquis within the passes; he retired to his home district with Xu Xuan and died there.

sentences.zh: 劉恭為更始報殺謝祿，自系獄，赦不誅。

translations.literal: Liu Gong for Gengshi avenged killing Xie Lu, himself bound in prison, was pardoned not executed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong avenged Gengshi by killing Xie Lu, then gave himself up; the emperor pardoned him.

sentences.zh: 帝憐盆子，賞賜甚厚，以為趙王郎中。

translations.literal: The Emperor pitied Penzi, rewards and gifts very thick, made him Prince of Zhao’s gentleman-of-the-palace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took pity on Penzi, heaped gifts on him, and appointed him gentleman-of-the-palace to the Prince of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 後病失明，賜滎陽均輸官地，以為列肆，使食其稅終身。

translations.literal: Later sick blind, was granted Xingyang equal-transport office land, made row shops, caused him eat its tax for life.
translations.idiomatic: Later he went blind; the court granted him land at the Xingyang equal-distribution office for market stalls and let him live on the rents for life.

translations.literal: 【Appraisal】
translations.idiomatic: Appraisal

sentences.zh: 贊曰：聖公靡聞，假我風雲，始順歸歷，終然崩分。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Shengong without renown, borrowed our wind and clouds, at first compliant returned to the calendar, in the end thus collapsed and split.
translations.idiomatic: The appraisal runs: Shengong rose from obscurity on borrowed wind and cloud; he first bowed to the calendar of fate, then fell apart in ruin.

sentences.zh: 赤眉阻亂，盆子探符。

translations.literal: Red Eyebrows obstructed chaos; Penzi drew the tally.
translations.idiomatic: The Red Eyebrows seized a season of turmoil; Penzi drew the lot that made him emperor.

sentences.zh: 雖盜皇器，乃食均輸。

translations.literal: Although stole imperial regalia, then fed on equal-transport.
translations.idiomatic: They stole the throne, yet in the end Penzi lived on the income of a tax office.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Collation notes

sentences.zh: 四六七頁一０行 共攻離鄉聚 按：殿本考證萬承蒼謂離鄉聚地名，章懷注非。

translations.literal: Page 467 line 10: jointly attacked Lixiangju. Note: the Dian edition’s textual research by Wan Chengcang says Lixiangju is a place-name; Prince Zhanghuai’s commentary is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Page 467, line 10, “jointly attacked Lixiangju”: Wan Chengcang in the Dian edition’s research argues that Lixiangju is a placename and Prince Zhanghuai’s gloss is mistaken.

sentences.zh: 今據加標號。

translations.literal: Now according to this add punctuation marks.
translations.idiomatic: Punctuation has been added on this basis.

sentences.zh: 四六八頁一三行 及其支黨朱鮪張卬等北入南陽號新市兵 按：校補引張熷說，謂王常傳卬與王常、成丹皆為下江兵，與紀異。

translations.literal: Page 468 line 13: and their branch party Zhu Wei, Zhang Ang, and others north entered Nanyang, called themselves Xinshi troops. Note: the collation supplement cites Zhang Reng’s view, saying in Wang Chang’s biography Ang with Wang Chang and Cheng Dan were all Lower Yang troops, differing from the annals.
translations.idiomatic: Page 468, line 13, on Zhu Wei, Zhang Ang, and allies entering Nanyang as the Xinshi force: the collation supplement cites Zhang Reng, who notes that Wang Chang’s biography classifies Ang with Wang Chang and Cheng Dan as Lower Yang troops, which disagrees with the annals.

sentences.zh: 四六九頁一０行 前鍾武侯劉望起兵 按：集解引通鑑考異，謂前書王莽傳「劉望」作「劉聖」。

translations.literal: Page 469 line 10: former Marquis of Zhongwu Liu Wang raised troops. Note: collected glosses cite Zizhi tongjian kao yi, saying in the Former Han’s Wang Mang biography ‘Liu Wang’ is written ‘Liu Sheng.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 469, line 10, “former Marquis of Zhongwu Liu Wang raised troops”: the collected gloss cites the Zizhi tongjian kao yi, which records the Former Han Wang Mang biography as reading “Liu Sheng” instead of “Liu Wang.”

sentences.zh: 四七０頁 六行 觸北宮鐵柱〔門〕 據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 470 line 6: struck the Northern Palace iron pillar [gate]: according to the Ji and Dian editions supplied.
translations.idiomatic: Page 470, line 6, “struck the iron pillar gate of the Northern Palace”: the character “gate” is supplied from the Ji and Dian editions.

sentences.zh: 按：續志有「門」字。

translations.literal: Note: the continuation treatise has the character ‘gate.’
translations.idiomatic: Note: the Xu Han zhi includes the word “gate.”

sentences.zh: 四七０頁一一行 俛首刮席不敢視 按：惠棟補注本「視」上有「仰」字。

translations.literal: Page 470 line 11: bowed head scraped mat did not dare look. Note: Huidong’s supplemented commentary edition has the character ‘raise’ above ‘look.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 470, line 11, on bowing and scraping the mat without daring to look up: Huidong’s supplemented commentary inserts the character for “lift the gaze” before “look.”

sentences.zh: 四七一頁 四行 驃騎大將軍宋佻為潁陰王 按：集解引惠棟說，謂光武紀及通鑑「宋」皆作「宗」。

translations.literal: Page 471 line 4: Cavalry-in-Attendant Grand General Song Tiao as Prince of Yingyin. Note: collected glosses cite Huidong, saying the Guangwu annals and Zizhi tongjian both write ‘Zong’ for ‘Song.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 471, line 4, naming Song Tiao as Prince of Yingyin: the collected gloss cites Huidong—the Guangwu annals and Zizhi tongjian both read the surname as Zong, not Song.

sentences.zh: 四七一頁 八行 陰平縣屬廣漢國 按：校補謂前漢陰平國屬東海郡，後漢改縣，屬同。

translations.literal: Page 471 line 8: Yinping county belonged to the state of Guanghan. Note: the collation supplement says in Former Han Yinping state belonged to Donghai commandery; in Later Han it was changed to a county; affiliation same.
translations.idiomatic: Page 471, line 8, “Yinping county under Guanghan”: the collation supplement notes that in Former Han Yinping was a state in Donghai commandery and was later demoted to a county under the same administration.

sentences.zh: 又前漢陰平道屬廣漢郡，後漢分屬廣漢屬國，注據陰平道言，雖亦可言「縣」，但屬前漢言，不當言「國」，屬後漢言，當云「屬國」，亦不當僅言「國」。

translations.literal: Moreover Former Han Yinping circuit belonged to Guanghan commandery; Later Han partitioned it to Guanghan dependent state; the commentary according to Yinping circuit speaks—although also can say ‘county,’ yet speaking of Former Han one ought not say ‘state’; speaking of Later Han one ought say ‘dependent state,’ also ought not only say ‘state.’
translations.idiomatic: Former Han’s Yinping circuit lay in Guanghan commandery; Later Han placed it in the Guanghan dependent state. The gloss follows the old name of the circuit—‘county’ is tolerable—but for Former Han one should not call it a ‘state,’ and for Later Han one should say ‘dependent state,’ not merely ‘state.’

sentences.zh: 四七二頁 五行 軍帥將軍 按：刊誤謂「帥」當作「師」，是時多置軍師，鄧禹傳亦作「軍師將軍」。

translations.literal: Page 472 line 5: army-commander general. Note: the errata says ‘commander’ should be written ‘army-master’; at that time many army-masters were appointed; Deng Yu’s biography also writes ‘army-master general.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 472, line 5, “army-commander general”: the errata substitutes the first character of the compound so it reads “army-master general,” matching widespread usage of that title and Deng Yu’s biography.

sentences.zh: 四七二頁一五行 捕賊掾 按：刊誤謂案前書合作「賊捕掾」。

translations.literal: Page 472 line 15: bandit-catching clerk. Note: the errata says checking the Former Han text it should jointly read ‘bandit-catching clerk.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 472, line 15, “bandit-catching clerk”: the errata argues the Former Han text transposes the first two graphs so the office reads “bandit-catching clerk” in that order.

sentences.zh: 四七二頁一六行 孟子對

translations.literal: Page 472 line 16: Mencius replied
translations.idiomatic: Page 472, line 16: “Mencius replied” (citation fragment).

sentences.zh: （梁惠）

translations.literal: (Liang Hui)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical restoration: (King Hui of Liang).

sentences.zh: 〔齊宣〕王曰 據殿本改。

translations.literal: [Qi Xuan] king said: according to Dian edition changed.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to “[King Xuan of] Qi said,” following the Dian edition.

sentences.zh: 四七三頁一二行 戰於蓩鄉 按：續志「蓩」作「務」。

translations.literal: Page 473 line 12: fought at Maoxiang. Note: the continuation treatise writes ‘Mao’ as ‘Wu.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 473, line 12, “battle at Maoxiang”: the Xu Han zhi writes the place name with a different homophone character (the wu graph instead of the mao graph).

sentences.zh: 四七三頁一四行 其

translations.literal: Page 473 line 14: its
translations.idiomatic: Page 473, line 14: the pronoun qi (fragment of a collation string).

translations.literal: (Mao)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical restoration: the mao syllable in the place name.

sentences.zh: 〔地〕蓋在今虢州湖城縣之閒 集解引王補說，謂「其蓩」通鑑注作「其地」，是。

translations.literal: [land] probably between today’s Guozhou and Hucheng county. Collected glosses cite Wang’s supplement, saying ‘its Mao’ in Zizhi tongjian commentary is written ‘its land’—correct.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss supplies “ground” or “site”: the location probably lay between modern Guozhou and Hucheng. Wang’s supplement notes that Hu Sanxing’s Zizhi tongjian commentary reads “its territory” instead of echoing the place name, which is correct.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this changed.
translations.idiomatic: The text has been emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 四七六頁 三行 復徙封

translations.literal: Page 476 line 3: again moved enfeoffment
translations.idiomatic: Page 476, line 3: “again transferred the fief” (citation fragment).

translations.literal: (Guan)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical restoration: the element Guan (wrong graph in the fief name; read as part of Huoze).

sentences.zh: 〔濩〕澤侯 據集解引錢大昕說改，注同。

translations.literal: [Huo] Marsh Marquis: according to collected glosses citing Qian Daxin’s view changed; commentary likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to “Marquis of Huoze,” per Qian Daxin in the collected gloss; the commentary is adjusted to match.

sentences.zh: 四七八頁 六行 次卒

translations.literal: Page 478 line 6: next clerk
translations.idiomatic: Page 478, line 6: “next … clerk” (citation fragment).

translations.literal: (clerk)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical reading: the graph for clerk or underling.

sentences.zh: 〔史〕 刊誤謂「吏」當作「史」。

translations.literal: [scribe]: errata says ‘clerk’ should be written ‘scribe.’
translations.idiomatic: The errata changes the office title graph from clerk to scribe.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this changed.
translations.idiomatic: The text has been emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 四七八頁 六行 汎相稱曰

translations.literal: Page 478 line 6: generally mutually called saying
translations.idiomatic: Page 478, line 6: “they generally addressed one another as …” (citation fragment).

translations.literal: (minister)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical reading: the graph for minister or subject.

sentences.zh: 〔巨〕人 刊誤謂前書言盜賊擅稱巨人，今此為臣人，亦誤也。

translations.literal: [great] man: errata says the Former Han speaks of bandits presumptuously calling themselves ‘great men’; now this is ‘minister-man’—also error.
translations.idiomatic: The errata restores “great”: the Former Han text has rebels styling themselves “great men”; the graph for minister here is a copyist’s error for great.

sentences.zh: 當作「巨」。

translations.literal: Ought to be written ‘great.’
translations.idiomatic: It should read the graph meaning great.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this changed.
translations.idiomatic: The text has been emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 四八０頁一０行 屬右校卒

translations.literal: Page 480 line 10: belonged to right camp clerk
translations.idiomatic: Page 480, line 10: “attached to the right camp …” (citation fragment).

translations.literal: (clerk)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical reading: the graph for clerk or underling.

sentences.zh: 〔史〕劉俠卿 據刊誤改。

translations.literal: [scribe] Liu Xiaqing: according to errata changed.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to “scribe Liu Xiaqing,” per the errata.

sentences.zh: 四八０頁一一行 唯盆子與茂及前西安侯劉孝最為近屬 按：沈家本謂按前書王子侯表，西安侯漢東平思王孫，而城陽近屬無封西安者，亦無名孝者。

translations.literal: Page 480 line 11: only Penzi and Mao and former Marquis of Xi’an Liu Xiao were closest kin. Note: Shen Jiaben says checking the Former Han princes and marquises table, the Marquis of Xi’an was a descendant of Prince Si of Dongping, yet among Chengyang close kin none was enfeoffed at Xi’an, also none named Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Page 480, line 11: Shen Jiaben observes that the Former Han table places the Xi’an marquis under the line of the Prince of Dongping posthumously titled Si, while no Chengyang kinsman held a Xi’an fief and none bore the personal name Xiao—so the “closest kin” line is problematic.

sentences.zh: 四八二頁 三行 衛尉諸葛稚聞之 按：「稚」原訛「釋」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 482 line 3: Commandant of the Guards Zhuge Zhi heard it. Note: ‘Zhi’ was originally miswritten ‘Shi’; straightway according to Ji and Dian editions corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Page 482, line 3: the personal name Zhi was miscopied as Shi and is corrected from the Ji and Dian editions.

sentences.zh: 四八二頁 九行 肴亦亂也 按：殿本「肴」作「殽」。

translations.literal: Page 482 line 9: yao also means chaos. Note: Dian edition writes ‘yao’ as ‘xiao.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 482, line 9, gloss on the word for confusion: the Dian edition writes an alternate graph with the same sense of mingled disorder.

sentences.zh: 校補謂殿本注作「殽」，取與正文相應。

translations.literal: Collation supplement says Dian edition commentary writes ‘xiao,’ taking to correspond with main text.
translations.idiomatic: The collation supplement notes that the Dian commentary uses that alternate graph to align with the main text.

sentences.zh: 然觀下文「肴亂日甚」，正文本作「肴」，知此處正文作「殽」，乃繙刻之誤，注蓋本不誤也。

translations.literal: Yet observing below ‘yao confusion daily more severe,’ main text originally writes ‘yao,’ know this place main text writes ‘xiao’—then is reprint engraving error; commentary probably originally not wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Below, the main text consistently uses one graph in “disorder grew worse”; the other graph in this line is a reprint error, while the commentary’s form was probably right all along.

sentences.zh: 四八二頁一一行 幽閉殿內掘庭中蘆菔根 按：汲本「內」作「門」。

translations.literal: Page 482 line 11: shut in hall inside dug courtyard turnip roots. Note: Ji edition writes ‘inside’ as ‘gate.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 482, line 11: the Ji edition reads gate for inside in the phrase about being shut within the halls.

sentences.zh: 御覽九八０引「掘」作「拔」。

translations.literal: Imperial Readings scroll 980 cites ‘dig’ as ‘pull up.’
translations.idiomatic: Taiping yulan 980 quotes pull up for dig.

sentences.zh: 又按：「閉」原訛「閑」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Also note: ‘shut’ was originally miswritten ‘idle’; straightway corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the graph for shut was miscopied as the graph for idle and has been corrected.

sentences.zh: 四八三頁一二行

translations.literal: Page 483 lines 1–2
translations.idiomatic: Collation locator: page 483, lines 1–2.

translations.literal: (de)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical reading: the graph de meaning obtain—an erroneous reading here.

sentences.zh: 〔後〕二十餘日 集解引王補說，謂袁紀、通鑑並作「後二十餘日」，是。

translations.literal: [after] more than twenty days: collected glosses cite Wang’s supplement, saying Yuan ji and Zizhi tongjian both write ‘after more than twenty days’—correct.
translations.idiomatic: Wang’s supplement restores the word after: both the Yuan ji and Zizhi tongjian read “some twenty days later,” which is adopted here.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this changed.
translations.idiomatic: The text has been emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 四八四頁 四行 廣一寸半 按：殿本「一寸」作「二寸」。

translations.literal: Page 484 line 4: width one inch half. Note: Dian edition ‘one inch’ as ‘two inches.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 484, line 4, “one and a half inches wide”: the Dian edition reads “two inches” instead of “one inch.”

sentences.zh: 四八五頁一五行 攻破城邑 按：刊誤謂案文當云「攻城破邑」。

translations.literal: Page 485 line 15: attack break cities. Note: errata says checking the text ought say ‘attack cities break settlements.’
translations.idiomatic: Page 485, line 15: the errata prefers the phrase order attack cities and break settlements over the transmitted wording attack-break cities.

sentences.zh: 四八六頁 七行 說文曰錚錚金也 按：說文「錚，金聲也」，此疑誤。

translations.literal: Page 486 line 7: Shuowen says zheng-zheng is metal. Note: Shuowen ‘zheng, metal sound,’ this suspected error.
translations.idiomatic: Page 486, line 7: the gloss that the Shuowen defines ringing metal is doubtful—the Shuowen defines the word as the sound of metal, not metal itself.
